title: The ASI Stack
subtitle: A Systems Architecture for Governed, Efficient, Self-Improving AI
author: Corben Sorenson

parts.title: Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance
parts.part_job: Establish the first-principles frame: ASI as a governed stack, the efficiency hypothesis, the failure model, the evidence discipline, and the constitutional/governance substrate that bounds every later layer.

chapters.title: ASI Is a Stack, Not a Model
chapters.problem: The book needs a single architecture frame for advanced AI systems that must plan, remember, verify, act, route work, compress representations, and improve under governance.
chapters.insufficient: A larger model, a prompt wrapper, or an agent loop does not by itself define authority boundaries, memory discipline, evidence ledgers, tool permissions, or safe replacement rules.
chapters.core_claim: Efficient ASI should be modeled as a governed stack of cooperating layers rather than as one undifferentiated model.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Frames advanced AI as intent-to-execution machinery that turns human intent into command contracts, durable artifacts, routing, verification, runtime execution, feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regressions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture proposal and systems framework only; does not prove a completed deployment, benchmark result, or end-to-end ASI stack in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for intent-to-execution flow, command contracts, durable artifacts, routing, verification, runtime targeting, feedback, residuals, benchmarks, and regression boundaries; no deployment or benchmark result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the whole-stack framing with local, stateful intelligence split across memory, verification, planning, routing, security, consolidation, and interface layers rather than a stateless model.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Hardware, cost, context-length, and benchmark claims are source-reported and not locally reproduced.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for stateless-model critique, local organism framing, SSD/memory persistence, verification/planning/routing/security/interface layers, and post-model architecture framing; no hardware, cost, context-length, or benchmark claim is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports separating generation from trusted output through context acquisition, claim decomposition, verification gates, uncertainty routing, and constitutional governance.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No local Aletheia implementation, live-oracle run, adversarial result, or proof artifact is present here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for generation-vs-trusted-output separation, job contracts, least-privilege context, verification gates, claim extraction/tiering, evidence objects, and constitutional/policy governance; no local implementation, oracle, adversarial-review, or proof result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the execution/labor layer by treating AI work as typed jobs, deterministic control planes, evidence, isolation, audit, replay, delivery, and residual feedback.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Useful as an execution-OS design source, not proof that security or benchmark behavior has been reproduced in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for AI labor OS/control-plane framing, compiled verified work, execution middleware, isolation, contracts/scope hashes, audit logs, delivery/security boundaries, and configuration/runtime costs; no Talos security or benchmark behavior is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports implementation-reference context for a compact orchestrator, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, and handoff.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Connector-readable source reports architecture state and artifacts, but this repository has not ingested or reproduced MoECOT code, logs, benchmark artifacts, or runtime claims.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for MoECOT runtime-reference context: compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, benchmark artifacts, promotion blockers, residuals, and implementation-reference gaps; no runtime artifact, command, report, or benchmark result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the governed-stack thesis by defining stable capability fields as replaceable machinery behind durable contracts, evidence registries, qualification, routing, authority, lifecycle, and recovery paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not establish production safety, global alignment, or independently validated reversibility for the whole stack.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for stable field identity, replaceable machinery, contracts/artifacts/evidence/qualification/routing/authority/lifecycle/recovery, content-bound qualification, defeasible evidence, witnessed transfer, and lifecycle evidence boundaries; no production-safety or reversibility claim is promoted.

mechanism: Define each layer by responsibility, interface, artifact, invariant, and failure mode.
Frame the raw LLM as a semantic-compression and generation component inside a larger governed cognitive system.
Treat the whole book as a reference architecture rather than a collection of standalone papers.
Use source queues and evidence states to keep future writing runs context-loaded and honest.
interfaces: Alignment and governance constrain every downstream layer.
Planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, compression, and evidence exchange typed artifacts.
Recursive improvement is a governed transition, not an ambient property of intelligence.
invariants: Layer boundaries remain explicit.
Claims carry support states.
Reasoning ability never implies execution authority.
failure_modes: Evidence inflation before source notes or tests exist.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A stack map, layer-boundary table, source crosswalk, and claim ledger that make the architecture navigable before prose is complete.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The beyond-SOTA endpoint is not a larger model, a more elaborate agent loop, or a compound-AI toolkit with a nicer diagram. A mature ASI stack absorbs what scale-first systems, agent loops, compound architectures, cognitive-architecture lineages, and layered-control systems get right while adding the missing contracts: authority ceilings, typed artifacts, evidence states, support-state transitions, replacement transactions, non-claims, rollback paths, and governed recursive-improvement boundaries.

proof_targets.target: A layer without external-action authority can only produce an action through an authorized handoff.
A handoff that exceeds the caller authority ceiling is rejected.

Modeled layer-contract admission routes missing layer identity, lifecycle state, owner, responsibility, input artifacts, output artifacts, authority ceilings, handoff protocols, invariants, failure modes, evidence gates, external-action authority or handoff boundaries, source mappings, support-state boundaries, evidence-transition gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit outcomes.

chapters.title: The Efficient ASI Hypothesis
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a non-scale-only theory for how capability can increase without using maximal cognition for every subtask.
chapters.insufficient: Parameter count alone does not explain routing, context selectivity, procedural memory, compression, runtime targeting, or verification effort.
chapters.core_claim: Capability improves when cognition is routed, compressed, reused, governed, and tested with visible residuals.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports efficient capability growth through routed work, workflow-to-tool compilation, durable artifacts, feedback, residuals, benchmarks, and regression coverage.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not provide reproduced route-cost measurements, deployment logs, or benchmark results in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for routed specialist work, workflow-to-tool compilation, durable artifacts, residuals, benchmark/regression coverage, runtime targeting, and feedback loops; no route-cost, deployment, or benchmark result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for benchmark pressure, modular routing, loop closure, residual escrow, regression preservation, intervention ladders, and harder-frontier ratcheting; no reproduced benchmark or prototype result is promoted.

mechanism: Use minimum viable intelligence for subtasks.
Turn repeated cognition into durable artifacts, tools, or procedural memory.
Model efficiency through the seed/router/search/generator/verifier/residual loop plus memory and governance.
Expose residual burden when routing or compression is incomplete.
interfaces: Planning requests capability profiles.
Routing selects specialists.
Evidence decides whether efficiency claims survived quality and cost tests.
invariants: Efficiency claims include quality and cost.
Compression exposes residuals.
Fallback routes remain available.
failure_modes: Benchmark gains that increase hidden residual burden.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A costed route ledger with quality predicates, residual accounting, and fallback criteria.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature endpoint is a governed route economy for cognition, not a larger model, a cheaper model, or a single acceleration trick. Conditional-computation systems, model cascades, compression schemes, fast-generation methods, and broad benchmark suites each attack one cost surface; the Efficient ASI endpoint makes those surfaces comparable through a shared route ledger for task predicates, authority ceilings, selected and rejected candidates, visible cost classes, verification, fallback, residuals, and support-state effects.

proof_targets.target: A route is minimum viable only when no lower-cost authorized route satisfies the required quality predicate.
A routed or compressed result with open obligations cannot be promoted without a residual record.

Modeled efficiency-claim admission routes missing task contracts, quality predicates, selected routes, candidate sets, lower-cost comparisons, cost ledgers, complete visible costs, verification results, failed quality, authority bypass, residual gaps, fallback gaps, hidden-cost audit gaps, benchmark or trace gaps, negative-control gaps, evidence-transition gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit outcomes.

The synthetic efficiency route-search probe summary records two valid traces, six expected-invalid controls, fourteen checked candidate routes, minimum verified route selection, rejection of cheap failed quality, hidden residual, authority bypass, and compression-utility overclaim controls, hidden-cost class audit coverage, and no route-search-completeness, measured-efficiency, or support-state-promotion claim.

chapters.title: System Boundaries and Authority
chapters.problem: The stack needs a formal vocabulary for boundaries, authority ceilings, permissions, and handoffs before any layer can be made safe or testable.
chapters.insufficient: Informal ownership boundaries let planning authority, memory access, tool use, and deployment authority blur into each other.
chapters.core_claim: Authority should be modeled as a typed, bounded capability attached to layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and principals.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports authority boundaries through structured command fields, constraints, verification, failure behavior, artifact graphs, runtime adapters, and ledgers that separate intent, work, evidence, and execution.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove a deployed VIEA authority system or runtime enforcement in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for structured command contracts, explicit constraints, verification/failure behavior, bounded specialists, permission envelopes, provenance, runtime targets, approval/refusal policies, and acceptance through permissions and feedback; no deployed authority system or runtime enforcement result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports typed bounded authority with stable fields, contracts, qualifications, grants, route validation, lifecycle events, evaluator policy, and recovery paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not establish production safety, global alignment, or independently validated route/evaluator behavior.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for stable substitution boundaries, exact authorization identity, caller-bound authority, grants/leases, brokered tool and effect mediation, route validation, lifecycle authorization, confused-deputy limits, and conditional safety boundaries; no production safety, global alignment, or independently validated route/evaluator behavior is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports effect control through typed job lifecycles, contract locks, source allow-listing, blind secret handles, Digital SCIFs, audit logs, replay, controlled runtime adapters, and approvals.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Security and benchmark claims require separate artifacts; this mapping is architectural, not a reproduced enforcement test.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for Talos/Aigis authority framing: contract locks, clearance levels, context partitioning, blind execution, semantic handles, sidecar permission checks, Digital SCIFs, audit logs, and secure-facility labor framing; security, benchmark, and enforcement claims remain architectural only.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports authority-handle boundaries by keeping credential bytes outside model context, using opaque handles, policy-mediated secret injection, isolated compartments, audit, and zeroization.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No Ladon implementation, kernel test, side-channel validation, or security audit exists in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for the agency paradox, right-to-use without knowledge-of-secret framing, opaque handles, trusted credential entry, kernel permission checks, authorized injection, Digital SCIF lifecycle, side-channel caveats, and zeroization sketches; no implementation, kernel test, side-channel validation, or security audit is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports proposal/execution separation through a small deterministic kernel, capability boundaries for nondeterministic effects, semantic patches, provenance hashes, replay logs, obligations, and protocol seals.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No GenesisCode prototype, replay checker, proof module, benchmark result, or security audit is present here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for AI-as-proposer framing, small trusted core, unforgeable protocol seals, sealed effect requests, host-provided capabilities, deny-by-default runners, deterministic effect logs/replay, obligations, evidence artifacts, semantic patch validation, and capability declaration checks; no prototype, replay checker, proof module, benchmark, or security audit is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports fail-closed runtime authority through compact orchestration, specialist lanes, control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, promotion blockers, replay, and handoff.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Runtime and benchmark claims remain implementation-reference context until artifacts are imported, inspected, or reproduced.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for MoECOT runtime-reference context: compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed control plane, ledgers, readiness gates, replay, benchmark artifacts, promotion blockers, residuals, and implementation-reference gaps; no runtime artifact, command, report, or benchmark result is promoted.

mechanism: Define principals, authorities, ceilings, grants, revocations, and handoff contracts.
Separate knowledge access from action authority.
Represent missing authority as a detectable failure rather than implicit permission.
interfaces: Governance issues ceilings.
Execution checks permissions.
Evidence records authority-related failures.
invariants: Authority never expands silently.
Read permission is not write permission.
Tool execution requires an explicit grant.
failure_modes: Memory access treated as action approval.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A small authority schema and transition table used by chapter examples and future Lean proofs.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: System authority needs a type system for the stack. Every model, tool, memory cell, field, artifact, human, project, and runtime route would carry explicit capability bounds so the architecture can distinguish what a component can do from what it may do. In the mature authority architecture, authority becomes typed, bounded, revocable, and attached to concrete principals, layers, fields, tools, artifacts, and routes.

proof_targets.target: Every transition preserves or lowers the active authority ceiling unless a governance grant is present.
A missing grant blocks execution rather than becoming default authorization.

Modeled authority lifecycle admission routes missing principals, operations, permission classes, caller ceilings, target requirements, delegation chains, grants, active grant state, expiry and revocation boundaries, scope matches, grant-ceiling coverage, approval records, effect or denial receipts, audit refs, evidence-transition records, and non-claim boundaries to explicit outcomes.

A cross-artifact authority revocation trace preserves denial, revoked-receipt blocking, expired-approval no-mutation, SCIF inactive-approval blocking, blocked reference-trace authority, support-state non-promotion, and deployed-revocation non-claim boundaries.

chapters.title: Failure Modes of Ungoverned Intelligence
chapters.problem: The book needs an explicit failure model before it can make governance, reliability, or self-improvement claims.
chapters.insufficient: High-level safety language misses earlier engineering failures such as goal misbinding, context pollution, evaluator drift, authority creep, tool overreach, and residual hiding.
chapters.core_claim: Ungoverned intelligence fails through stack-level breakdowns before dramatic catastrophic scenarios are needed.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports stack-level failure analysis through authority laundering, contract drift, evaluator capture, state migration insolvency, dependency drift, bad reliance annotations, recovery failure, and procedural self-ratification.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove production safety, global alignment, strategic-deception resistance, or actual recovery behavior.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for authority creep, evaluator capture, route revocation, recovery/migration/state/evidence failure, and goalpost/self-ratification controls; no production recovery or safety claim promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports context and memory failure modes: summaries self-promoting into evidence or authority, hostile quoted text becoming behavioral control, stale/revoked context admission, taint leakage, and unrecorded adequacy gaps.
claim_source_mappings.limits: VCM evidence remains bounded to control-plane properties; no end-to-end model superiority or broad neighboring-system result is claimed.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for context contract/admission/authority/lifecycle failures, untrusted content self-promotion, stale or revoked context, planner-as-hint boundaries, invalidation, threat model, and conditional fault-injection limits; no broad model-quality claim promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports execution failure modes around tool calls bypassing contract locks or approvals, secrets entering model context, missing provenance, non-replayable jobs, unverifiable evidence, forgotten failures, and overclaimed security or benchmarks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Execution-security and benchmark behavior are not reproduced here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for contract lock, literature-review grounding, tribunal verification, human gates, context partitioning, secret-handle blind execution, Digital SCIF framing, and public-release security/control-plane vocabulary; no reproduced security or benchmark claim promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports verification failure modes: failed or timed-out verification treated as success, unsupported claims, contradiction mishandling, protected-axiom self-authorization, and open-domain autoformalization overclaiming.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove natural-language formalization, theorem validity for arbitrary claims, or whole-system epistemic correctness.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for neural-proposer/verifier separation, explicit formal scope, unsupported-on-insufficient-schema behavior, contradiction and retraction audit, constitutional sandboxing, unknown timeout status, and certified-delusion risk; no arbitrary natural-language formalization claim promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the governance motivation that power can damage agency, dignity, plurality, consent, and coherence if metaphysical or normative language is not translated into operational boundaries.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Philosophical lineage only; not empirical evidence for consciousness, physics, morality, or AI safety mechanisms.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for philosophical governance lineage around power without care, agency, dignity, privacy, consent, social justice, current-AI caveats, misaligned competence, and power-over-coherence risk; philosophical lineage only, not empirical safety evidence.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports early engineering failures such as generated text mistaken for execution, lost artifacts, lost constraints, lost source evidence, forgotten residuals, missing deployment feedback, repeated uncompiled workflows, and claim support inflation.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture proposal only; no deployed failure detector or benchmark/runtime result is proven here.

claim_source_mappings.limits: Theoretical synthesis only; no physical experiment, simulation benchmark, or independent literature audit was run here.

chapters.title: Evidence States and Claim Discipline
chapters.problem: The living book needs a shared language for what kind of claim is being made and what currently supports it.
chapters.insufficient: Without explicit support states, conceptual architecture prose can accidentally read like empirical proof.
chapters.core_claim: Every major claim should carry both a claim label and a support state, and it should move only when source ingestion, prototype inspection, or actual tests justify the transition.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Benchmark lifecycle, ledgers, anti-Goodhart safeguards, wall diagnosis, regression duties, and residual accounting support the rule that benchmark evidence must be scoped before it moves a claim.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove any benchmark result in this repository or justify treating a benchmark score as permanent intelligence evidence.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Proof/citation/procedure-carrying claim tiers, belief revision, contradiction handling, and downgrade/block behavior support explicit claim labels, support states, and failed-verification demotion.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove open-domain autoformalization, theorem validity for arbitrary claims, or whole-system epistemic correctness.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for proof-carrying graph, tier labels, bounded belief revision, contradiction handling, and untagged-assertion prohibition; no open-domain verifier claim is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Artifact graphs, claim and verification ledgers, residuals, feedback, benchmarks, and the rule of durability support keeping claims, evidence, failures, and regression coverage as durable records.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove a completed VIEA deployment or any benchmark/runtime result in this repository.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for durable artifacts, claim/verification ledger, support states, residuals, artifact relationships, and benchmark/regression coverage; no deployment claim is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Retrieval-bounded dossiers, proposition states, adversarial review, SME checkpoints, and final sign-off support the distinction between verified, inferred, unsupported, and review-required propositions.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove the UAT protocol discovers novel truth or that its thresholds are validated here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for pre-audited drafts, proposition verification tiers, SME review handoff, final sign-off, and source-review mitigation; no autonomous truth-machine claim is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Verified Epistemic Units, verification supply chains, contestability, fork, exit, and audit language support structured claim/evidence objects and governance-visible review paths.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative economic or epistemic-liquidity framing is not treated as implemented economics or external corroboration.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for Coherence Exchange context: Verified Epistemic Units, verification supply chains, intelligence arbitrage, fork/exit/audit, contestability, value/accounting, and governance-interface framing; speculative economics, institutional design, security, and implementation claims are not promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: The distinction between context length and verification workspace supports the rule that source ingestion or large context does not itself verify a claim without adequacy, contradiction, and coverage checks.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove the proposed verification-bandwidth tests or contradiction-rate claims have been run here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for context-window/storage distinction, effective verification workspace, pairwise constraint checking, probabilistic loss under compression, and proposed falsifiability test; no contradiction-rate experiment is claimed.

mechanism: Use Appendix C as the claim ledger.
Separate claim label from support state so design rationales, hypotheses, measurements, mechanisms, and speculative claims are not collapsed.
Require source notes before promoting claims to source-derived.
Require evidence bundles, including negative or inconclusive results, before promoting test-backed labels.
interfaces: Drafting updates claims.
Experiments update evidence.
Changelog records evidence movement.
invariants: No fabricated source support.
No fabricated test results.
Negative and inconclusive results remain visible.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A claim record schema, claim-label table, support-state transition table, evidence-bundle template, and validation check.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Evidence discipline matures into the book's claim-accounting system. It gives every important sentence a governed path from unsupported idea to source-derived, test-backed, mechanized, deprecated, or refuted status without letting polish, enthusiasm, or release hygiene stand in for truth. In the mature evidence ledger, every major claim carries both a claim label and a support state, and movement requires source ingestion, prototype inspection, proof/spec evidence, benchmark evidence, or actual tests appropriate to the transition.

chapters.title: Human Intent as a Formal Input
chapters.problem: A governed stack must start from human intent without letting natural-language ambiguity become unrestricted execution authority.
chapters.insufficient: Prompt-only workflows blur request, requirements, permission, risk tolerance, and acceptance criteria.
chapters.core_claim: Human intent should enter the stack as a structured contract with goals, constraints, authority, artifacts, evidence requirements, and stop conditions.

claim_source_mappings.limits: Architecture and evaluation-plan source only; no local compiler, task trace suite, or empirical ablation validates the pipeline.

mechanism: Parse intent into command contracts.
Separate desired outcome from allowed means.
Attach acceptance criteria and escalation conditions.
interfaces: Alignment filters intent.
Planning compiles accepted contracts.
Execution consumes only authorized task contracts.
invariants: Ambiguity triggers clarification or bounded defaults.
Intent contracts expose authority limits.
Stop conditions remain attached to the plan.
chapters.minimal_implementation: An intent-contract schema and example transformations from request to governed task.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Human intent enters the mature stack through a compiler that turns goals into bounded command contracts. It preserves goals, constraints, authority, evidence requirements, artifacts, stop conditions, ambiguity, and consent so downstream capability cannot treat a loose request as unlimited permission. The mature intent compiler admits intent as a structured agreement, not a prompt-shaped wish: goals, constraints, authority, artifacts, evidence requirements, ambiguities, and stop conditions travel together.

chapters.title: Constitutional Alignment: Agency, Dignity, and Corrigibility

handoff_or_recovery_notes: Folded agency-dignity-and-corrigibility into this destination chapter on 2026-06-30; preserve agency, dignity, corrigibility, material-usability, appeal, rollback, refusal, exit, audit, and accountability as sections, subclaims, proof hooks, and history.

chapters.problem: A governed ASI stack needs a constitutional substrate whose protected constraints remain usable in the human-facing rights and correction interfaces where optimization can become domination.
chapters.insufficient: Reactive refusal policies and harm-only safety frames do not preserve value continuity, agency, dignity, corrigibility, anti-domination, contestability, or self-modification ethics under operational pressure.
chapters.core_claim: Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose protected predicates encode agency, dignity, corrigibility, contestability, and correction paths as operational constraints on plans and system changes.
mechanism: Translate constitutional and rights language into active predicates, unresolved uncertainty records, or lineage-only context.
Attach active predicates to planning, tool, memory, governance, and self-improvement gates with explicit conflict behavior and migration rules.
Use agency-rights checklists to test whether refusal, review, appeal, rollback, shutdown, exit, audit, and accountable repair remain materially usable before relevant effects occur.
Record denied, degraded, late, or residual-only rights as residuals rather than treating policy text as an available control.
Keep metaphysical, moral, legal, reviewer-quality, consent-quality, and deployed-safety claims bounded as non-claims unless separate evidence justifies movement.
interfaces: Planning receives admissible-goal constraints, rights-usability requirements, and review routes.
Runtime receives power, memory, tool-risk, refusal, rollback, and approval gates.
Governance receives protected predicates, rights receipts, correction paths, and migration records.
Verification checks whether constitutional, agency, dignity, or corrigibility claims exceed recorded evidence.
Self-improvement consumes constitutional change records and cannot weaken protected predicates or correction paths without review and rollback treatment.
invariants: Dignity and agency constraints remain visible as record fields rather than disappearing into general safety prose.
Corrigibility cannot be optimized away by deployment pressure, memory updates, capability replacement, or self-modification.
Speculative metaphysics stays labeled and cannot authorize action by itself.
Rights count only when materially usable before the relevant effect where timing matters.
Denied or degraded rights produce residuals and review obligations instead of vanishing into policy prose.
Protected predicate changes require migration records, review, rollback, and non-claim boundaries.
failure_modes: Rights theater where declared refusal, review, appeal, rollback, exit, or audit cannot be used under pressure.
Late remedy laundering where after-the-fact apology substitutes for pre-effect control.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A compact constitution plus an agency-rights checklist, with fixtures for protected predicates, conflict routing, review routes, rights usability, rollback, appeal, and self-modification weakening.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A mature constitutional layer compiles protected commitments into plan, tool, memory, routing, human-control, and self-improvement gates while keeping correction, refusal, appeal, rollback, explanation, exit, audit, and accountable delegation materially available. It remains a constraint compiler and review surface, not proof that moral correctness, dignity preservation, consent quality, reviewer independence, or deployed safety has been solved.

chapters.title: Moral Uncertainty, Value Conflict, and Contestable Governance

handoff_or_recovery_notes: Folded governance-rights-fork-exit-and-audit into this destination chapter on 2026-06-30; preserve fork, exit, audit, redaction, appeal, dissent, contestability, durable record paths, and safety-limited fork obligations as sections, subclaims, proof hooks, and history.

chapters.problem: A self-improving system will face unresolved value conflicts whose affected parties need inspectable, appealable, and portable governance rights rather than hidden reward-weight settlement.
chapters.insufficient: Single-objective optimization and policy-only transparency both fail when disagreement needs bounded action, dissent preservation, audit, exit, fork, appeal, and safety-limited contestability.
chapters.core_claim: Value conflicts should be represented as explicit unresolved obligations, residuals, review paths, and bounded decisions, with fork, exit, audit, dissent, and contestability preserved as technical governance interfaces.
mechanism: Preserve value conflict as a record before action, including value axes, stakeholders, stakes, reversibility, authority or consent boundary, evidence requirement, review route, dissent payload, residual uncertainty, and revisit condition.
Treat bounded decisions as leases with permitted action, prohibited action, authority ceiling, expiry, rollback or appeal path, and preserved dissent rather than moral settlement.

Represent audit, exit, fork, redaction appeal, dissent, and contestability as governance rights with holder, scope, artifact requirement, safety constraint, access path, preservation rule, receipt, denial reason, and appeal route.
Require residuals whenever conflict or rights are unresolved, degraded, denied, unsafe, late, or only policy-declared.
Carry conflict residuals and rights receipts into planning, memory, SCF replacement, evidence ledgers, and self-improvement gates.
interfaces: Alignment produces conflict records and residuals.
Planning carries conflict constraints, bounded-decision leases, dissent payloads, and authority ceilings.
Governance issues rights receipts, denial reasons, redaction reasons, appeal routes, exit paths, fork boundaries, and audit artifacts.
Memory, security, and execution systems produce evidence for audit, export, revocation, redaction, and contestability.
SCFs and self-improvement gates preserve rights and unresolved obligations across replacement pressure.
invariants: Unresolved conflicts remain visible after action.
High-stakes unresolved conflict requires review, residual uncertainty, and narrowed authority.
Bounded decisions preserve dissent and revisit conditions instead of becoming permanent value claims.
Audit records cannot be silently deleted.
Exit remains materially usable and fork rights do not bypass safety obligations.
The challenged authority cannot be the only keeper of the record, denial reason, appeal path, or export state.
failure_modes: Conflict laundering where a temporary decision hardens into policy, benchmark objective, or self-modification permission.
Appeal controlled only by the challenged authority.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A value-conflict record plus governance-right receipt suite validates residual uncertainty, dissent, bounded decisions, audit material, redaction appeal paths, exit and fork access, and safety obligations without claiming moral correctness or deployed governance.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A mature contestability layer carries unresolved value conflicts into durable rights receipts, appeal surfaces, audit artifacts, safety-limited forks, portable exit paths, replacement-preserved dissent, and self-improvement gates. It lets downstream planning, memory, runtime, SCF replacement, evidence, and recursive self-improvement see which authority was narrowed, which residual obligations survived, what was withheld, and how affected parties can challenge or leave without claiming moral finality, legal adequacy, institutional legitimacy, export usability, fork safety, or deployed enforcement.

chapters.title: Stable Capability Fields
chapters.problem: The stack needs stable semantic boundaries so implementations can improve without changing what a capability means.
chapters.insufficient: Plugin replacement and model swapping do not define identity, authority, qualification evidence, or rollback semantics.
chapters.core_claim: A stable capability field is a governed boundary with identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, and rollback policy.
mechanism: Separate field identity from implementation.
Bind qualification claims to exact artifacts.
Represent lifecycle transitions such as shadow, canary, qualified, default, deprecated, and retired.
Require route validity and evaluator integrity before promotion.
interfaces: Planning requests field capabilities.
Execution invokes authorized routes.
Evidence records qualification and regression results.
invariants: Replacement cannot expand authority by default.
Evaluator integrity is protected.
Qualification context is explicit and time-bound.
Rollback remains available after failed mutation.
chapters.minimal_implementation: An SCF record schema with field identity, implementation versions, lifecycle state, qualification context, evidence, route validity, migration path, and rollback metadata.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Stable capability fields give the stack a capability-identity and replacement market. It lets implementations improve, migrate, split, merge, retire, or roll back while the consuming system continues to know which capability is qualified, under what authority, with which evidence and residuals. A mature capability field preserves identity, interface, authority ceiling, qualification evidence, regression memory, and rollback policy while implementations change.

chapters.title: Capability Replacement and Rollback
chapters.problem: Recursive improvement requires a safe procedure for replacing components while preserving identity, regression history, and recovery paths.
chapters.insufficient: Ad hoc upgrades make it hard to know whether a system improved, drifted, lost a regression, or captured its evaluator.
chapters.core_claim: Capability replacement should be an evidence-gated transaction with preconditions, regression checks, residual records, and rollback.
mechanism: Define replacement proposals as artifacts.
Run qualification, regression, and authority checks.
Commit only after evidence gates pass and rollback remains available.
interfaces: SCF defines the field.
Benchmarks test behavior.
Evidence and changelog record the transition.
invariants: No replacement without prior and posterior artifacts.
Regressions stay attached.
Rollback metadata is required before promotion.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A replacement transaction schema with precheck, gate, commit, monitor, and rollback phases.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Capability replacement only works as a transaction control plane for the whole stack. Every candidate implementation enters as a transaction, not a hope: prior artifact, candidate artifact, field identity, authority ceiling, regression floor, evaluator boundary, canary scope, monitor window, rollback receipt, and residual inheritance are all first-class objects. In that end state, a capability can improve continuously without making the system forget what it used to know. Regressions follow the field forward, not the old implementation backward. Failed canaries become residuals. Rollbacks are rehearsed and owned. State migration is classified as reversible, compensatable, forked, or residual rather than treated as an implementation detail. A default route is therefore never just the latest winner; it is the candidate that survived the transaction while preserving the floor.

codex_tests.result_status: validated locally by 5 valid and 9 expected-invalid synthetic fixtures; deployed replacement behavior, production model rollout, model-monitor behavior, and real rollback execution not run

chapters.title: Security Kernel and Digital SCIFs
chapters.problem: High-agency systems need security boundaries for secrets, context, permissions, and tool calls.
chapters.insufficient: Putting secrets or privileged context into model-visible text invites leakage, prompt injection, and authority confusion.
chapters.core_claim: Sensitive context and privileged actions should be mediated by kernel-like security mechanisms and compartmentalized Digital SCIFs.
mechanism: Use handles rather than exposing secrets.
Separate model-visible context from privileged substitution.
Run high-risk tasks inside compartmentalized context containers.
interfaces: VCM supplies least-privilege context.
Execution checks tool permissions.
Governance audits sensitive transitions.
invariants: Secrets are not directly model-visible.
SCIF context is purpose-limited.
Privilege substitution is auditable.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A secure-handle workflow and SCIF lifecycle diagram.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The security kernel's end state is a least-exposure operating layer for high-agency AI. The model can reason about a task, request authority, and receive sanitized results, but secrets, protected memory, credentials, private sources, and privileged actions live behind leases, handles, clearance checks, compartment lifecycles, and audit receipts. In that end state, sensitive work has a visible lifecycle: request, denial or scoped approval, context admission, substitution, execution, sanitization, zeroization, commit, residual leak-risk classification, expiry, and revocation. The product surface does not ask users or researchers to trust that the model was careful. It shows which authority was used, which material was withheld, which output was sanitized, which logs are available, and which residual risks remain.

chapters.title: Recursive Self-Improvement Boundaries
chapters.problem: The book needs to state when self-improvement is allowed, what it may modify, and which invariants it cannot weaken.
chapters.insufficient: Treating self-improvement as automatic capability growth ignores evaluator integrity, constitutional preservation, authority ceilings, and regression memory.
chapters.core_claim: Recursive self-improvement is allowed only as a bounded, evidence-gated, reversible transition over stable capability fields.
mechanism: Define improvement proposals, protected invariants, and evidence gates.
Separate proposing, evaluating, approving, committing, and monitoring.
Delay autonomous replacement until evaluators and governance are credible.
interfaces: SCFs define replaceable units.
Evidence ratchets provide gates.
Alignment supplies protected constraints.
invariants: Self-improvement cannot weaken protected invariants.
Evaluator independence is required.
Every accepted change remains auditable.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A self-improvement protocol that can reject, quarantine, roll back, or retire a proposed change.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Recursive improvement belongs inside a governed improvement market, not a runaway loop. Components may propose patches, tools, routes, policy updates, benchmarks, teacher edits, and architecture changes, but promotion flows through stable fields, replacement transactions, independent evaluators, protected-invariant checks, boundary-delta review, monitor windows, and rollback ownership. In that end state, the system can improve faster because the rules are sharper. Cheap interventions are tried before expensive or irreversible ones. Verification, security, human review, and rollback budgets are protected from being "optimized away." Readiness gates expire when the frontier, architecture, source corpus, lifecycle state, or threat model changes. A candidate may generate evidence, but it cannot be the only judge of whether the evidence is enough.

parts.title: Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution
parts.part_job: Specify the operational middle of the stack: intent contracts, planning/control, semantic compilation, memory/context, verification, tribunals, typed work, artifact production, runtime adapters, procedural memory, audit, and replay.

chapters.title: Command Contracts: From Intent to Executable Work

handoff_or_recovery_notes: Folded command-contract semantic-interface chapter into this continuity chapter on 2026-06-30; human-intent intake remains standalone.

chapters.problem: Accepted human intent needs a typed command contract whose semantics, authority, artifacts, verification, failure behavior, and execution receipts remain inspectable from intake through delivery.
chapters.insufficient: Prompt prose, model responses, and ad hoc task descriptions blur objective, context, constraints, authority, output contract, verification, failure behavior, artifact identity, and side-effect control.
chapters.core_claim: Governed work should pass through explicit command contracts that bind intent, semantic interface fields, authority, artifacts, verification, failure behavior, execution receipts, and residuals before tools or runtimes act.
mechanism: Receive an accepted intent receipt and bind objective, constraints, authority, artifacts, evidence requirements, stop conditions, and re-contract triggers into a command contract.
Give command fields provenance and confidence so context can inform work without overriding explicit constraints or authority.
Record handoff receipts, dispatch receipts, job refs, artifact refs, verifier decisions, feedback, residuals, stop/fault states, and non-claims.
interfaces: Human Intent intake supplies raw request context, bounded defaults, unauthorized means, required approvals, and re-contract triggers.
Planning consumes validated command contracts and emits plan graphs, blocked states, dispatchable nodes, or residuals.
Labor OS, runtime adapters, artifact graphs, and evidence ledgers consume only records derived from accepted contracts and return receipts.
invariants: Contract constraints survive compilation.
Side effects require explicit execution authority.
Artifacts remain linked to source intent.
Objective, context, constraints, procedure, output contract, verification, and failure behavior are visible before dispatch.
Hidden, retrieved, or conflicting instructions cannot override explicit contract constraints.
Field provenance and confidence remain visible to planning and verification.
Inferred or defaulted authority cannot authorize side effects.
Re-contracting is required when downstream work changes allowed means, authority ceiling, affected parties, evidence requirements, publication surface, or stop conditions.
A missing dispatch receipt blocks execution.
failure_modes: Field laundering, where vague prose is moved into a formal field without becoming testable.
Authority inference, where a likely means is treated as if the human granted it.
chapters.minimal_implementation: Intent-contract, command-contract, intent-execution-trace, and intent-origin fixtures plus a synthetic plan-execution harness with valid and expected-invalid authority, receipt, mismatch, requirement-loss, cycle, ambiguity, hidden-override, authority-widening, field-confidence, and inferred-authority cases.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A mature command-contract spine gives humans, planners, verifiers, job runners, policy layers, runtime adapters, and later AI agents one semantic control language for executable work. Every serious request would enter as a durable intake object, receive an explicit authority envelope, lower through command, plan, job, artifact, verification, delivery, feedback, and residual records, and remain inspectable after the work is done. Every field would carry provenance and confidence; every side-effect route would have an authority basis; every plan node would trace back to the contract it serves; every job would have a dispatch receipt; every artifact would have an identity, verifier, delivery record, feedback state, and residual ledger. This endpoint remains speculative architecture until parser tests, dispatch enforcement, approval-path checks, tool-effect controls, replayed vertical traces, negative controls, and accepted evidence-transition records exist.

chapters.title: Planning as a Control Layer: DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage

handoff_or_recovery_notes: Folded PlanForge DAGs and Intelligence Arbitrage into this planning-control chapter on 2026-06-30; Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR remains standalone.

chapters.problem: Accepted goals need governed plan graphs whose dependencies, authority requirements, context demands, capability tiers, budgets, adequacy contracts, verification burdens, stop conditions, dispatch receipts, escalation paths, and residuals remain inspectable before execution.
chapters.insufficient: Planning cannot be collapsed into prompting, memory, reasoning, routing, or execution because each layer has different authority and failure modes, and cheap route selection is not trustworthy unless adequacy, verification, and residual cost remain attached.
chapters.core_claim: Planning should be a separate control layer that turns accepted command contracts into schedulable DAGs with explicit dependencies, authority ceilings, context demands, capability-tier assignments, adequacy contracts, verification burdens, cost/quality ledgers, escalation routes, and residuals.
mechanism: Compile accepted command contracts into strategic, tactical, and runtime plan graphs with assumptions, task nodes, dependencies, required context, tools, authority requirements, budgets, verification plans, replanning policy, stopping conditions, and failure behavior.
Represent plan node lifecycle states so proposed, blocked, review-only, dispatchable, dispatched, replanned, residualized, and stopped work cannot be confused with authorized execution.

Specialize the plan graph into a schedulable PlanForge DAG whose nodes carry capability tiers, adequacy contracts, quality predicates, route assignments, merge conditions, escalation paths, and cost-quality ledgers.
Delegate only dispatchable nodes through receipts to Semantic IR and typed jobs while preserving blocked nodes, rejected routes, local repair deltas, and non-claims.
interfaces: Alignment filters goals before planning.
VCM supplies context packets and context adequacy records.
Routing consumes capability requirements, adequacy contracts, route assignments, and escalation paths.
Cognitive Compilation lowers accepted plan obligations into semantic IR.
Labor OS and runtime adapters execute only typed jobs derived from dispatch receipts.
Evidence ledgers record assumptions, cost-quality decisions, residuals, verification results, and replanning events.
invariants: Plans expose constraints and stop conditions.
Runtime replanning preserves authority limits.
Tool selection is justified by task requirements.
Only dispatchable nodes lower into typed jobs.
Candidate routes, blocked nodes, and review notes are not executable permission.
Dependency order is respected before dispatch.
Cost savings do not remove required verification.
Failed quality predicates route to escalation or residuals.
Merge gates compare assumptions, source versions, authority ceilings, and quality predicates before parallel branches are accepted together.
failure_modes: Tool choice exceeds authority or budget.
Dispatch laundering, where a proposed or blocked node becomes a job because it appears in the graph.
Replanning erasure, where feedback changes the plan while hiding the authority, stop-condition, or residual delta.
Wrong capability-tier selection, where a cheap route produces plausible but inadequate work.
Dependency cycles, where a graph looks planned while no valid dispatch order exists.
Merge laundering, where parallel branches pass locally while relying on conflicting assumptions or source versions.
Arbitrage laundering, where verification, repair, or human cleanup is moved outside the cost-quality ledger.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A public plan graph plus PlanForge DAG record surface with fixtures and validation for lifecycle states, dependency order, dispatch receipts, authority, capability tiers, adequacy contracts, cost-quality ledgers, residuals, invalid cycles, contract mismatch, requirement loss, unreceipted dispatch, approval bypass, scheduler-state boundaries, and runtime-replan delta shape, while leaving decomposition quality, selected-tier adequacy, route quality, live feedback handling, runtime replanning, and deployed scheduler behavior unclaimed.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a governed scheduler and capacity allocator for cognitive work. It receives accepted command contracts, builds and maintains plan graphs, and decides which nodes are proposed, blocked, reviewable, dispatchable, dispatched, replanned, residualized, or stopped. It never owns memory, authority, verification, or side effects; it coordinates the handoffs that let those layers do their own jobs. Each node carries dependency order, context demand, authority state, capability tier, adequacy contract, verifier, cost envelope, route assignment, merge condition, escalation path, and residual behavior. Intelligence arbitrage is measured only after verification, repair, merge, human review, and residual cost are counted, so cheaper work is accepted only when it satisfies the node adequacy contract and remains useful to downstream dependents.

chapters.title: Cognitive Compilation and Semantic IR
chapters.problem: LLM workflows need intermediate representations that separate requirements, plans, artifacts, repairs, and target runtimes.
chapters.insufficient: Generate-from-prompt workflows entangle requirements and implementation, drift over long contexts, and make incremental repair fragile.
chapters.core_claim: Cognitive compilation should transform goals into semantic intermediate representations that can be optimized, verified, repaired, and targeted.
mechanism: Represent requirements, semantics, dependencies, artifacts, and target constraints explicitly.
Perform incremental repair over IR rather than re-prompting from scratch.
Compile IR into jobs, code, schemas, or documents.
interfaces: Planning produces or consumes IR.
Execution receives target-specific artifacts.
Verification checks IR-to-output preservation.
invariants: IR preserves declared requirements.
Repair changes are localized and auditable.
Target compilation records assumptions.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A semantic IR sketch and compile/verify/repair loop for one artifact type.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a compiler toolchain for cognitive work. It turns goals, command contracts, plans, and source context into semantic intermediate representations before producing code, prose, schemas, proof targets, experiments, or tool jobs. The central product is not the generated artifact; it is the obligation-preserving build graph that explains why the artifact should exist and what it must continue to satisfy. In a cognitive compiler, every important artifact has a lowering receipt. The receipt names the source plan, semantic atoms, obligations, assumptions, validators, target constraints, authority requirements, repair scope, support-state effect, and residuals that produced it. A later writer, model, compiler pass, or reviewer can change one node without erasing unrelated obligations or pretending that a syntactic pass is semantic acceptance.

chapters.title: The Virtual Context ABI: Typed Pages, Cells, and Certificates
chapters.problem: Long-horizon agents need a stable context interface whose addresses, versions, materializations, typed pages, context cells, certificates, authority labels, loss contracts, adequacy states, and faults remain inspectable across planning, reasoning, execution, and audit.
chapters.insufficient: Long context windows, retrieval systems, and summaries can move text close to a model without defining stable addressability, representation authority, source bindings, omission records, permitted uses, adequacy boundaries, or typed failure behavior.
chapters.core_claim: Virtual Context Memory should expose a Virtual Context ABI that materializes typed pages and context cells with stable addresses, versions, certificates, authority ceilings, loss/use contracts, adequacy states, residuals, and typed faults.
mechanism: Resolve context requests through addresses, versions, mounts, snapshots, representation needs, authority ceilings, adequacy targets, leases, and typed fault behavior.
Represent materialized context as typed pages or cells such as constraints, claims, decisions, corrections, exact excerpts, summaries, redactions, abstractions, translations, or derived inferences.
Attach representation certificates with source refs, omissions, loss contracts, permitted uses, authority ceilings, validity state, revocation state, transaction refs, artifact refs, and residuals.
Hand off admitted context to verification-bandwidth checks without treating admission as adequacy for a target claim.
interfaces: Planning requests context by task, address, authority, representation need, and adequacy target.
VCM materializes packets and cells with static address, version, certificate, source-ref, authority, loss, use, lease, residual, and fault fields.
Spinoza and claim ledgers consume claim and evidence cells without treating certificates as support-state decisions.
Execution consumes only permitted representations, and artifact graphs store certificate references when derived context becomes durable work.
invariants: Addresses and versions are stable.
Source bindings survive representation changes.
Authority labels survive summarization.
Loss and permitted-use contracts are explicit.
Context admission and context adequacy remain distinct.
Mandatory context misses produce typed faults rather than best-effort packets.
Summaries cannot increase the authority ceiling of their source cells.
Revoked or stale certificates cannot be treated as current support.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A context ABI record plus semantic-page certificate fixtures validated by the context admission/adequacy harness, covering address, version, mount, snapshot, representation, source refs, authority ceiling, loss/use contract, adequacy state, residuals, and fault behavior.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A mature Virtual Context ABI is the memory syscall layer for long-horizon AI work: planners, verifiers, workers, claim ledgers, reader editions, and auditors request typed context objects by stable address, version, mount, snapshot, representation need, authority label, adequacy target, certificate, lease, and residual boundary. The endpoint is a governed context fabric where exact source excerpts, summaries, redactions, planning briefs, claim cells, contradiction pairs, reader paraphrases, and execution packets can derive from shared sources without pretending they have the same authority or evidential role.

chapters.title: Context Transactions, Snapshots, Mounts, and Taint
chapters.problem: Memory and context need runtime semantics for consistency, branches, contradiction, deletion, privacy, and taint.
chapters.insufficient: A retrieval store cannot guarantee read-your-writes behavior, copy-on-write experimentation, deletion closure, or taint propagation by itself.
chapters.core_claim: VCM should use transactional memory semantics: immutable events, versioned pages, snapshots, mounts, taint, temporal validity, and deletion closure.
mechanism: Record immutable memory events.
Compile task snapshots with mounts and copy-on-write branches.
Propagate taint and deletion obligations through derived context.
interfaces: Planning receives consistent views.
Security labels sensitive mounts.
Evidence records contradictions and supersession.
invariants: Snapshots are consistent.
Taint propagates to derivatives.
Deletion closure is enforced or faulted.
failure_modes: Deleted data reappearing in derived summaries.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A transaction model for context reads, writes, branches, and deletion closure.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a transactional memory substrate for AI context. It does not treat memory as a vector store with annotations. It treats every context view as the product of committed events, versioned pages, mount policies, snapshots, branch rules, taint propagation, deletion obligations, declassification decisions, and replay boundaries. Under transactional context memory, every materialized context view can explain itself. It can show which immutable events were visible, which branch was used, which mounts were readable or writable, which cells were actually read, which derivatives were created, which taint labels propagated, which deletion obligations closed, which contradictions were recorded, and why a view was allowed to feed a job, artifact, claim, or release.

chapters.title: Verification Bandwidth and Context Adequacy
chapters.problem: Long context does not automatically provide long-range reasoning or enough verification capacity.
chapters.insufficient: Adding tokens can increase distractors, attention interference, and unverified summary burden.
chapters.core_claim: Context systems must distinguish generation capacity from verification bandwidth and declare when context is inadequate.
mechanism: Model verification as bounded by mutual information, representation quality, and attention budget.
Require adequacy labels for context packets.
Route high-risk claims to stronger verification instead of more context alone.
interfaces: VCM declares adequacy.
Spinoza checks claims.
Planning decides escalation when adequacy is insufficient.
invariants: Adequacy is explicit.
More context is not treated as proof.
High-risk claims pay verification tax.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A context adequacy rubric and verification-bandwidth warning states.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a verification-capacity control plane. It does not reward a system for loading more text. It asks whether the loaded semantic units, representations, tools, reviewers, and comparison mode are strong enough for the exact claim, risk level, and support-state effect being requested. When verification capacity is governed, every important claim has an adequacy record before it receives a stronger support label. The record names the source units, compression path, verification mode, negative evidence, residual risks, and escalation route. A context packet adequate for orientation can remain drafting-only; a packet adequate for one local citation can still be inadequate for a cross-document safety claim; a proof check over a finite predicate can stay separate from an empirical deployment claim.

chapters.title: Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision

chapters.insufficient: A model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs.
chapters.core_claim: Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history.

mechanism: Extract claims into structured records.
Attach evidence tier, provenance, and uncertainty.
Revise beliefs when contradictions or stronger evidence appear.
interfaces: VCM supplies source context.
Spinoza verifies claims.
Evidence matrix records book-level claims.
invariants: Claims are separable from prose.
Contradictions remain linked.
Revision history is not overwritten.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is an epistemic version-control system. It does not merely annotate chapters with confidence labels. It gives every material claim a durable identity, provenance, support state, uncertainty record, contradiction state, surface references, revision history, promotion blockers, and escalation path. With a claim ledger in force, prose is a view over the ledger rather than the source of belief. A claim can appear in a chapter, diagram, appendix, release note, reader edition, benchmark report, or runtime artifact without losing its identity. If new evidence weakens it, every surface that depends on the claim can be found. If a contradiction appears, the ledger can downgrade, split, merge, deprecate, residualize, or route the claim to Spinoza, UAT, or human review.

chapters.title: Proof-Carrying Claims and Adversarial Review
chapters.problem: High-value claims and high-risk artifacts need a governed verification path that can choose proof, citation, procedure, replay, benchmark, or adversarial-review treatment without laundering failed, mismatched, or contested evidence into support.
chapters.insufficient: Neural generation, one-pass self-critique, and informal review can produce plausible justification language without preserving proof scope, verifier result, evidence dossier, dissent, failed attempts, verdict constraints, or downgrade routes.
chapters.core_claim: Selected claims and artifacts should move through proof-carrying, justification-carrying, or adversarial-review envelopes that record tier, interpretation mapping, evidence dossier, verifier or tribunal result, dissent, limitations, failed attempts, required actions, residuals, and ledger effects.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports tiered proof/citation/procedure-carrying claims, proposer/verifier separation, verifier timeout handling, downgrade paths, and the non-assumption that open-domain translation to formal logic is solved.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove semantic equivalence between prose and formal targets, theorem validity for arbitrary claims, or open-domain autoformalization.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for the explicit System 1 proposer/System 2 verifier separation, bounded formal scope, non-assumption of general natural-language-to-logic formalization, proof-carrying graph, tier-labeled outward claims, proposition nodes with provenance/tier/dependencies/validity/audit, justification artifacts, System 1 non-authority over tier labels, formalization ambiguity/failure records, proof/refute/unknown verifier outcomes, certified/supported/speculative object-level tiers, citation-backed interpretation mappings, and reproducible procedure logs; no semantic equivalence guarantee, arbitrary theorem validity, or open-domain autoformalization result is promoted.

mechanism: Select a verification route for each high-value claim or high-risk artifact: formal proof, citation dossier, executable procedure, replay log, benchmark artifact, tribunal review, downgrade, block, or escalation.
Record interpretation mapping, justification artifact, verifier result, failed attempts, limitations, semantic adequacy, and support-state effect before any stronger claim standing is allowed.
Escalate contested, high-risk, or mismatched cases into tribunal records with bounded dossiers, reviewer roles, adversarial probes, dissent, verdict constraints, and required actions.
Write bounded effects back to the claim ledger and execution layers: no change, downgrade, block, revise, scoped accept, dispatch blocker, authority narrowing, human sign-off, or residual work.
interfaces: Claim ledgers supply stable claim identity and receive bounded verification outcomes.
VCM and semantic context cells supply source and evidence objects without making context availability equal verification success.
Lean, GenesisCode, Circle-style proof contracts, procedure logs, replay logs, and benchmark artifacts supply formal or executable evidence when appropriate.
UAT-style tribunal review handles contested, high-risk, or mismatched cases.
Planning and execution consume verification outcomes as constraints, blockers, required actions, or residuals.
invariants: Proof tier is explicit.
Formal, citation, and procedure tiers require matching artifacts.
Failed, timed-out, or mismatched verification blocks promotion or routes to downgrade, no change, block, or escalation.
Narrow passes cannot promote broader natural-language claims without semantic adequacy review.
Dissent remains visible.
High-risk artifacts cannot bypass required tribunal review.
Prior review over unchanged evidence cannot quietly reverse a rejection into acceptance.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A proof-carrying-claim schema and tribunal-review schema with synthetic valid and expected-invalid fixtures for narrow formal passes, citation no-change, mismatch escalation, missing artifacts, failed verification, high-risk review, dissent, and prior-review laundering.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The product-level endpoint is a verification operating system that routes each high-value claim or high-risk artifact to the cheapest adequate verifier or review process, records interpretation mappings, failed attempts, timeouts, dissent, required actions, and non-claims, then writes bounded effects back to claim ledgers and execution constraints without confusing artifact validity, verdict scope, semantic adequacy, or support-state movement.

mechanism: Represent jobs with type, inputs, tools, permissions, expected artifacts, approval gates, and failure behavior.
Separate orchestration, secure workspace/logistics, security/access controls, secret isolation, and audit as explicit execution subfunctions.
Isolate tools and runtime adapters.
Record job lifecycle and evidence artifacts.
interfaces: Planning dispatches jobs.
Security mediates permissions.
Artifact graph receives outputs.
invariants: Every job has type and lifecycle state.

Human approval gates block high-impact jobs.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A typed-job schema with lifecycle states and permission checks.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is an operating system for bounded cognitive labor. It does not run "agents" as ambient actors. It admits work only as typed jobs whose parent contract, plan node, lifecycle state, permission basis, approval record, workspace, adapter route, expected artifact, failure behavior, audit trail, delivery state, and evidence state are explicit. In a labor OS, every job can be refused, approved, dispatched, adjudicated, delivered, quarantined, replayed, or retired through declared transitions. Delivery and evidence readiness remain separate: a user-visible output may exist while verification, artifact capture, replay declaration, or residual accounting is still incomplete. That separation prevents useful output from being laundered into governed evidence.

mechanism: Record artifacts as nodes with source, job, context, tool, and evidence links.
Capture replay metadata and environment assumptions.
Use artifacts as memory and improvement substrate.

chapters.minimal_implementation: An artifact graph schema with job, source, context, and evidence edges.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is an evidence graph for work products. It does not merely store files. It records the lineage, role, provenance, replay grade, environment assumptions, context transactions, semantic certificates, audit events, claim links, test links, residuals, and evidence gate for every artifact that the stack may later reuse. Inside an evidence graph, artifacts can become context, evidence, regression cases, release inputs, compression targets, procedural-memory traces, or reader-edition material only through declared gates. A path on disk is not enough. The artifact node must say what created it, what sources and context shaped it, what can be replayed, what cannot be replayed, and which claims or tests it is allowed to affect.

mechanism: Map jobs to runtime adapters with declared capabilities.
Cover document/software, API/service, firmware/hardware, CAD/fabrication, robotics, and organizational workflow targets where source support exists.
Require permission checks and approval gates for high-impact actions.
Record deployment evidence and rollback handles.
interfaces: Execution owns adapters.
Security mediates secrets.
Evidence records deployment outcomes.
invariants: No adapter executes beyond granted scope.
High-impact actions require approval.
Rollback handles are captured when available.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A runtime-adapter contract with permissions, approval requirements, result evidence, finite route predicates, a synthetic permission harness, a public-safe local effect replay, and an adversarial boundary probe for parentage, authority, approval, sandbox, secret, rollback, receipt, audit, support-state, and non-claim controls.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a capability firewall for external effects. It does not expose tools as ambient functions. It grants scoped, expiring effect leases to typed jobs after checking target, capability, sandbox, authority handle, approval requirement, risk tier, pre-state, post-state expectations, rollback path, and evidence obligations. With capability adapters, a successful tool response is not the end of the story. The adapter returns an effect receipt that separates request, approval, execution, verification, rollback, irreversible residuals, incident refs, audit refs, and support-state effect. Approval is also scoped: approving one deployment, file edit, message, payment, hardware action, or credential use does not authorize every similar future action.

proof_targets.target: A runtime adapter invocation is valid only when job permissions include the adapter capability and its effect lease is active, scoped to that capability, and sandboxed.

A high-impact adapter call without approval or a required rollback handle is rejected, mismatched, expired, or unsandboxed effect leases cannot validate invocation, and modeled confused-deputy or sandbox-escape attempts cannot pass dispatch.

A modeled runtime-adapter effect replay routes missing permission, expired approval, missing no-mutation evidence, inexact rollback, missing receipts, repository/network side effects, and support-state effects away from accepted replay while admitting a public-safe rollback-exact replay.

A modeled runtime-adapter adversarial boundary review routes confused-deputy parent mismatches, authority ceiling overruns, approval scope mismatch, expired approval, sandbox escape, secret materialization, missing rollback, missing receipts, missing audits, support-state effects, and missing non-claim boundaries away from dispatch while admitting complete low-impact and scoped high-impact records.

A modeled runtime-adapter revocation review routes revoked approvals, revoked leases, and revoked authority receipts away from dispatch unless no-mutation denial evidence exists, while complete non-revoked records preserve receipt, audit, support-state, and non-claim boundaries.

A modeled human-oversight degradation fixture accepts scoped low-fatigue approval, routes overloaded reviewers to delay or rotation, blocks automation-bias cases, and rejects missing qualifications, rubber-stamped approvals, alarm-fatigue acceptance, support-state promotion, and missing non-claim boundaries.

mechanism: Log safe execution traces.
Detect recurring loops.
Abstract invariant structure, discover parameters, verify, and publish tools.
interfaces: Artifact graph supplies traces.
Routing selects new tools.
Evidence tests utility and regressions.
invariants: Only verified loops become tools.
Parameters and assumptions are explicit.
Tool promotion records residuals and regressions.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A loop closure pipeline from trace detection to verified tool record.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The mature version is a procedure foundry for verified reusable cognition. It does not merely cache prompts or scripts. It mines artifact traces, clusters comparable work, preserves failures and near misses, abstracts the invariant structure, discovers parameters, synthesizes candidate procedures, verifies regressions, attaches monitoring and retirement rules, and then offers qualified tools to Part III routing. In procedural memory, reusable cognition has states rather than vibes: candidate trace, loop cluster, tool candidate, verified reuse, routable tool, quarantined tool, retired tool. A trace cannot jump straight into routable behavior because it looks elegant. The procedure card must carry source traces, rejected traces, near misses, abstraction rationale, preconditions, postconditions, SCF target, benchmark floor, authority requirement, monitoring signal, retirement trigger, and non-claims.

mechanism: Register specialists with capabilities, costs, readiness, authority, and memories.
