MoECOT runtime packets extend route receipts with command refs, specialist lanes, control-plane gates, ledgers, replay refs, handoff refs, denied routes, failed gates, missing replay refs, source-reported fields, reproduced fields, blocked fields, and non-claims.
SCFs govern whether a routed runtime lane can become a default route or replacement implementation.
invariants: Specialist authority is local.
Routing decisions are logged.
Fallback paths remain available.
Implementation evidence is separated from design argument.
Runtime promotion follows readiness gates.
Control-plane gates fail closed when evidence, authority, or replay is missing.
A source-reported benchmark artifact is not a reproduced book result.
failure_modes: Treating implementation branding as architecture proof.
Specialist lanes mutating authority without control-plane approval.
Replay theater that preserves successful paths while omitting failed gates, denied routes, human interventions, missing sources, and residual cases.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A router registry with capability metadata, cost, authority, and fallback rules.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Routing becomes strategic when it is a governed capability market inside the stack. It is not a bigger tool picker. It is a control boundary that can select among specialists, proof checkers, retrieval lanes, local runtime cores, human review, and fallback routes while preserving the authority, evidence, and residual obligations attached to the choice. In the mature routing market, route selection is a governed lease, not a suggestion engine: a lightweight head selects bounded specialists only with the authority, context, tools, fallback, and residual ownership the task permits.

chapters.minimal_implementation: A module lifecycle state machine and residual escrow ledger.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Readiness is the lifecycle control plane for every routable or replaceable capability in the stack. It is the mechanism that lets the architecture improve without confusing novelty, benchmark wins, or source-reported success with permission for ordinary use. In the mature readiness control plane, readiness is a lifecycle state with evidence, residuals, expiry, allowed routes, blocked routes, rollback consequences, and review ownership.

proof_targets.target: A module can enter promoted or stronger readiness states only after required gates and finite lifecycle records for fresh evidence, residual escrow, fallback, expiry, regression floor, authority scope, and route permission are present.

A quarantined, superseded, retired, stale, or incomplete readiness transition cannot be used for ordinary promotion without the required fallback, residual, rerun, supersession, or retirement records.

The deterministic synthetic readiness lifecycle probe summary records candidate-to-shadow, shadow-to-canary, default-ready, quarantine-with-fallback, supersession-with-residual, and retirement-with-receipt paths plus rejected negative controls, support-state non-promotion, and non-claim boundaries.

chapters.title: Personal Compute Hives and Federated Edge Intelligence
chapters.problem: A governed ASI stack needs an owned compute substrate where personal, family, project, and rented devices can run work without collapsing reachability into authority.
chapters.insufficient: Cloud-only assistants, single-device apps, ordinary home clusters, and generic schedulers do not preserve identity, privacy, family authority, data locality, physical-tool risk, federation boundaries, and evidence records as one governed substrate.
chapters.core_claim: A Personal Compute Hive should turn trusted devices, portals, stores, workers, and temporary rented or project nodes into a federated, policy-bounded compute organism whose scheduler routes work by capability, locality, trust, cost, energy, and authority.

mechanism: Represent persons, devices, portals, workers, stores, datasets, tools, jobs, and external hives with signed identity and policy cards.
Classify jobs by data sensitivity, tool authority, physical-world risk, federation scope, cost, energy, and verification requirement before scheduling.
Let eligible devices bid on work after policy filtering, then choose the best lawful node rather than the fastest reachable node.
Treat phones, laptops, tablets, dashboards, and headsets as portals; desktops, NAS boxes, old machines, workshop computers, and rented nodes as bounded workers or stores.
Represent device roles, family-sensitive portals, public project jobs, and hive memory classes separately so owned substrate does not collapse into surveillance or botnet behavior.

Use portal cards, approval receipts, job bids, federation leases, sandbox manifests, artifact manifests, evidence bundles, payment or credit records, reputation records, and revocations as the Hive-to-Hive protocol surface.
Preserve audit records, data-placement decisions, approval events, and residuals for every cross-device or cross-hive action.
interfaces: VCM declares what context is needed and what data may move.
Talos and PlanForge lower tasks into typed jobs with resource, authority, and verification requirements.
Octopus/RMI routing and TokenMana-style resource records score eligible devices and rented nodes.
Field-of-God and SCF governance define family, child, privacy, physical-tool, and federation membranes.
Project hives and artifact stewards interact through explicit job contracts, sandbox manifests, evidence bundles, and revocation paths.
invariants: Reachability is not authority.
Policy filtering precedes optimization.
External work receives sandboxed job slots, not private network access.
Family and child-facing portals preserve dignity, consent boundaries, and parent/guardian review for sensitive transitions.
Secrets remain handles and approvals, not strings in model context.
Every temporary federation link has an expiration, revocation path, and audit record.
failure_modes: Personal hive becomes a botnet or malware execution surface.
Rented or public project compute leaks private data.
Wrong-device scheduling drains batteries, overheats machines, exposes data, or bypasses approvals.
Identity confusion grants child, guest, agent, or project authority to the wrong principal.
Hive memory becomes an ungoverned swamp of artifacts, logs, models, and personal data.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A local personal-hive dashboard with a device registry, portal cards, signed resource cards, job queue, manual phone approval receipts, policy-first scheduler, job bids, federation leases disabled by default, artifact log, and no public federation or autonomous spending.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Personal compute hives become valuable as an owned compute fabric for personal, family, project, and rented capacity. It lets the ASI Stack place work near the right data and device without turning reachability into authority, convenience into surveillance, or cheap compute into a privacy leak. In the mature personal-hive fabric, owned and rented devices form a policy-bounded compute fabric only when scheduling respects capability, locality, trust, cost, energy, data class, physical risk, and authority.

proof_targets.target: A hive scheduler admits a job only when identity, data, tool, federation, and approval policy checks pass before optimization.
A faster node cannot be selected when its policy membrane forbids the job's data, authority, or network scope.
A high-risk hive job that requires approval cannot execute unless a bound approval receipt is present.
External hive access requires an active lease with scope, sandbox, evidence, expiration, and revocation records.

A structured hive-work admission review routes malformed jobs, missing identity/data/tool policy, registry gaps, scheduler-policy gaps, high-risk jobs without portal approval, external access without leases or sandbox records, missing cost or energy budgets, missing dropout plans, missing audit receipt plans, missing residual owners, and support-promotion attempts without evidence transitions into explicit outcomes.

A partitioned-authority fixture quarantines partitioned stale-grant revocation cases, requests fresh authority receipts after healing, rejects stale-grant dispatch and mutation without no-mutation evidence, preserves residual ownership, and blocks support-state promotion while making no deployed partition-tolerance claim.

open_evidence_gaps: Appendix C now records exact passage-reviewed mappings for all assigned Personal Compute Hives sources; support remains at argument because the new hive admission harness is synthetic record validation only and no live device registry, policy-first scheduler, portal approval service, federation protocol, family-governance policy engine, rented-node sandbox, or deployed behavioral tests exist.

Record-shape schemas, valid fixtures, narrow finite Lean predicates, and the synthetic hive admission harness have been implemented; no personal hive scheduler, network overlay, device registry, approval portal, rented-node sandbox, federation run, energy measurement, dropout recovery run, privacy guarantee, or security result has been executed from this repository.
External tooling sources are reviewed as adjacent substrate references only; they do not prove the ASI Stack hive design or any safety property.

chapters.title: Compact Generative Systems: Generate, Verify, Repair, and Residual Honesty

handoff_or_recovery_notes: Folded generate-verify-repair-compression into this destination chapter on 2026-06-30 through the conservative compression merge; preserve GVR receipt states, exactness/repair/fallback accounting, consumer policy, proof hooks, tests, source mappings, and history.

Folded semantic-representation-and-tree-structured-models into this destination chapter on 2026-06-30 as a Semantic Representation Leasing section; preserve semantic node records, grounding/adequacy/interoperability gates, proof hooks, fixtures, source mappings, restoration conditions, and history.

chapters.problem: Compact generators and generate-verify-repair receipts need one claim-accounting surface so useful compression does not hide residual burden, exactness debt, repair cost, fallback, or consumer-policy limits. Semantic representations add the same risk at the meaning layer: a clean graph, tree, or token can look efficient while hiding provenance loss, hierarchy drift, consumer-policy mismatch, or residual uncertainty.
chapters.insufficient: Seed-size, compression-ratio, and generated-reconstruction claims can hide verification, repair, fallback, downstream utility, metadata, interface, governance, and human-review costs. Semantic graphs and typed meaning structures are also insufficient by themselves when they lack provenance, task-scoped adequacy, interoperability records, supersession, fallback, and residual honesty.
chapters.core_claim: Compact generative systems should store the smallest useful governed generator plus the cheapest exact or scoped residual, while preserving verification, fallback, consumer policy, and residual-burden records.
mechanism: Define compact generative cores and their residuals through a compactness ledger.
Use the seed/router/search/generator/verifier/residual loop as the default compression-control model.

Treat generate-verify-repair as the stricter exactness lane: generated regions, verifier results, repair residuals, literal fallback, proxy-rate status, final-rate status, and consumer policy travel in one receipt.
Treat the raw LLM as a compressed generative engine, not as the entire intelligent system.
Separate lawful generation from exact remainder and scoped loss.
Record where compactness, exactness, consumer policy, or rate accounting fails.

Fold semantic representation into the compression control plane as Semantic Representation Leasing: graph nodes, tree paths, and semantic tokens may carry work only with provenance, grounding state, permitted uses, consumer policy, adequacy limits, interoperability records, residual uncertainty, versioning, supersession, and quarantine or fallback behavior.

Keep semantic-node lifecycle states alongside compact-generation and compression-receipt states so proposed, grounded, adequate-for-task, interoperable, superseded, stale, and quarantined meaning objects cannot silently become source authorities.
interfaces: Compression feeds memory and routing through compactness ledgers and compression receipts.
Evidence tests downstream utility, exactness, declared loss, and consumer-policy boundaries.
Procedural memory turns repeated generation into tools only when residuals and verification remain visible.
Resource economics counts generation, verification, repair, metadata, interface, fallback, and human-review costs before efficiency claims.

Semantic Node Records can feed VCM pages, Spinoza claim graphs, planning, cognitive compilation, compression records, and human explanation surfaces only within declared permitted uses and consumer policies.
invariants: Lossy claims are labeled.
Residual burden is visible.
Fallback exists when compact generation or generated reconstruction fails.
Verification precedes exactness claims.
Repair cost and interface cost are counted before compression savings are claimed.
Consumer use is scoped to the reconstruction contract, declared loss, and support-state boundary.
Compactness does not promote support state without a separate evidence-transition record.
Semantic nodes are grounded or labeled speculative.
Hierarchy changes preserve prior references or record supersession.
Shared semantic graphs remain indexes and working representations, not independent source authorities.
Consumer use is scoped by grounding, adequacy, interoperability, residual uncertainty, and permitted-use fields.
failure_modes: Verification skipped or scoped to the wrong contract.
Consumer-policy leakage from preview or lossy use into proof, audit, benchmark, citation, or training use.
Proxy-rate drift after final serialization erases the apparent saving.
Degenerate governance where the compact core evaluates its own adequacy.
Recursive instability when compact structures modify the system that defines their success.
False explainability, where a clean semantic path is cited instead of the source, proof, or test it was meant to index.
Canonical graph capture, where a semantic graph becomes an unreviewed authority source.
Hierarchy drift or stale consumer mappings, where downstream systems keep using superseded node meanings.
Semantic laundering, where compact meaning structures hide uncertainty, provenance loss, or task-specific inadequacy.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A compact generative record plus compression receipt fixture suite with one residual-burden case, one lossy or blocked exactness case, one failed-verification case, one repair-or-fallback case, and one consumer-policy rejection. The folded semantic-representation slice adds `semantic_node_record.valid.json` as a record-shape fixture for provenance, hierarchy, relation refs, tokenization contract, grounding state, versioning, supersession, residual uncertainty, permitted uses, evaluation refs, and consumer-policy limits; it remains record-shape validation only.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A mature compression control plane treats compact generators, generated reconstructions, exact repairs, scoped residuals, consumer policies, fallback, replay, and resource accounting as one audited representation market. It lets the stack choose the cheapest lawful representation for a task while preserving verifier quality, repair burden, exactness boundaries, declared loss, fallback availability, and non-claims. This remains a target architecture until public codec, verifier, utility, rate, and replay evidence exists. The mature compression layer also leases semantic representations. Graph nodes, tree paths, and semantic tokens become task-scoped meaning objects whose provenance, grounding, adequacy, interoperability, supersession, consumer policy, and residual uncertainty travel with every use. They may reduce burden only when they preserve the constraints required by the consumer and fall back to fuller context, source artifacts, or quarantine when they do not.

proof_targets.target: A compact representation with unresolved obligations carries residual records.
A lossy representation cannot be marked exact without verification evidence.

A modeled compact-generation admission review routes missing source artifacts, compression boundaries, residual records, lossy exactness overclaims, reconstruction evidence, fallback paths, verifier-cost records, semantic provenance, hierarchy-migration records, evidence-transition gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit outcomes.

The compact GVR synthetic slice has a finite Lean fixture bridge matching the literal baseline, selected compact receipt, lossy exactness control, negative-rate/no-fallback control, bounded-search-overrun control, and selected-versus-baseline byte relation.
An exact reconstruction claim requires generator output plus repair residual to equal the target.
A failed verification blocks exactness promotion.
A semantic node marked grounded has at least one provenance link.
A hierarchy update preserves prior node references or records supersession.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the compact-generative core claim through the seed/rule system/memory-state/residual/error/verification/governance tuple, smallest-adequate-structure framing, generative leverage, residual burden, verification cost, governance power, and hidden-complexity debt metrics. Folded generate/verify/repair lineage: Supports residual accounting and governance around compressed generation through compact seeds, rule systems, residual/error channels, verification contracts, governance interfaces, and hidden complexity debt. Folded semantic-representation lineage: Supports compact semantic substrates only when compression, generation, residuals, verification, governance, hidden complexity, and permitted use remain explicit.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Conceptual framework and research program only; no local CGS benchmark, utility test, proof of compact adequacy, generator, or implementation has been run here. Folded GVR boundary: Does not establish that any generate-verify-repair codec preserves utility, improves rate, or reconstructs data in this repo. chapters.title: Fast Generation Architectures

handoff_or_recovery_notes: 2026-06-24 browser-GPT fast-generation planning note; use as author-intent and literature-queue context only.

chapters.problem: The stack needs to reduce serial token-generation latency without treating raw tokens per second as intelligence or bypassing verification.
chapters.insufficient: Standard autoregressive decoding, raw throughput benchmarks, and isolated serving optimizations do not specify when a faster generation mode is acceptable, how it is verified, or how its failures block promotion.
chapters.core_claim: Fast generation should be treated as a governed, planner-selected choice among autoregressive, speculative, multi-token, diffusion, early-exit, state-space, KV-cache, and hybrid modes selected by task risk, quality target, latency budget, compute, memory, and verification burden.
mechanism: Separate raw generation speed from effective verified tokens per second and useful solution per second.
Let PlanForge select a generation mode from task requirements, risk tier, latency budget, context shape, and verifier availability.
Use draft, verify, repair, fallback, and benchmark records before promoting any accelerated mode.
Route VCM context packets into the selected generator and route accepted outputs through Spinoza, Talos, Benchmaxxing, and SCF gates.
Treat multi-seed diffusion and hybrid AR, MTP, and diffusion systems as research hypotheses until source-specific experiments or stronger evidence transitions exist.
interfaces: PlanForge chooses the generation mode.
VCM supplies the bounded context packet.
The generator emits draft tokens, sketches, spans, or candidates.
Spinoza or a verifier checks acceptance predicates.
Talos turns accepted output into artifacts.
Benchmaxxing records speed-quality evidence.
SCF governs whether a mode is promoted, quarantined, or left experimental.
invariants: No fast generation mode is promoted on raw tokens per second alone.
Every accelerated route names its generation mode, verifier, acceptance predicate, risk tier, and fallback.
Accepted output is counted separately from proposed output.
High-risk tasks can require slower verified generation even when a faster mode exists.
Speculative, diffusion, MTP, and hybrid claims remain at argument level until source-specific tests, reproduced results, or accepted external-literature support justify promotion.
failure_modes: Speed hides lower answer quality or higher repair cost.
The verifier becomes the true bottleneck.
Draft heads, diffusion sketches, or cached continuations drift out of distribution.
Memory bandwidth, KV-cache pressure, or parallel sampling cost erases token-speed gains.
Multi-seed generation increases sample count without improving useful solution per second.
Fallback is omitted after rejection or uncertainty.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A generation-mode registry and benchmark record with task risk, latency budget, context packet, mode, draft source, verifier, acceptance predicate, accepted tokens, wall-clock time, quality or pass result, compute and memory notes, fallback, and promotion decision.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The endpoint for fast generation is a governed generation-mode control plane. It lets the stack choose among slow, fast, speculative, parallel, recurrent, cached, and hybrid generation routes without allowing raw throughput to masquerade as capability or evidence. In the mature generation-mode controller, faster generation becomes routable only when task risk, accepted-output quality, verifier cost, fallback rate, latency, compute, memory, and serving pressure are measured together.

proof_targets.target: A fast generation route records generation mode, verifier, acceptance predicate, risk tier, and fallback before promotion.
Raw tokens per second cannot promote a fast generation claim without accepted or verified token evidence, task-success evidence, and a baseline.

A modeled fast-generation admission review routes missing mode records, context packets, risk tiers, quality targets, verifiers, acceptance predicates, baselines, accepted outputs, verifier costs, fallback and residual handling, high-risk override gaps, budget gaps, evidence-transition gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit outcomes.

The public Project Theseus generation-mode import fixture matches a finite Lean summary fixture and proves the imported accepted-span speed-lift warnings do not permit promotion without useful-solution evidence, promotable comparisons, all hard boundary gates passing, and zero missing report refs.

The public-safe fast-generation task-bundle fixture preserves baseline and candidate task quality, shows candidate deterministic cost-accounting improvement over baseline, rejects the latency-only negative control, and blocks support promotion within the finite result summary.

chapters.title: RankFold, NeuralFold, and Artifact Compression
chapters.problem: The stack needs artifact-level and tensor-level compression strategies that remain honest about residuals and utility.
chapters.insufficient: Storage savings are not enough if compressed artifacts lose semantics, break downstream use, or require expensive fallback too often.
chapters.core_claim: RankFold/NeuralFold-style artifact compression belongs as a bounded implementation hypothesis with probe-route fallback and residual coding.
mechanism: Use low-rank residual coding or functional preprocessing where appropriate.
Probe compressed artifacts before use.
Route to full artifacts when probes fail.
interfaces: Artifact graph stores compressed and full references.
Routing selects representation by task.
Evidence records latency, ratio, and utility.
invariants: Fallback path is known.
Residual coding is measured.
Downstream utility is tested.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A compressed artifact record with ratio, residual, probe, fallback, and utility fields, plus a public-safe local RankFold replay probe and artifact-import record for synthetic roundtrip behavior and existing archive metadata/verifier summaries.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Artifact compression needs an admission system before compressed forms can enter the stack. It treats compressed forms as routed candidates that must earn each use case instead of inheriting the authority of the full artifact. In the mature artifact-admission lane, compressed artifacts are candidates for specific uses, not replacements for the original, until probes, decode behavior, residual coding, fallback, and consumer policy accept them.

chapters.title: Resource Economics and Token Budgets

handoff_or_recovery_notes: simulation-fidelity-and-physical-constraints folded into this chapter as Simulation Fidelity and Claim Transport on 2026-06-30; old public URL preserved through a static historical stub.

chapters.problem: Compute, context, verification, simulation fidelity, and human attention are scarce resources that the architecture must allocate explicitly.
chapters.insufficient: Ignoring resource economics or fidelity boundaries makes high-quality verification unaffordable, encourages synchronized overload or hidden cost shifts, and lets synthetic results travel farther than their contracts support.
chapters.core_claim: Resource governance accounts for token budgets, verification tax, load stability, and risk-adjusted inference value.
mechanism: Track task value, uncertainty, cost of error, inference cost, verification tax, protected overhead, and displaced costs.
Use regenerative or budgeted capacity mechanisms where useful.
Escalate verification when risk justifies the cost.
Separate aggregate serving throughput and memory pressure from single-request verified-output value.
Attach Simulation Contract Records when simulated, synthetic, benchmark, or scenario results are used as evidence.
Block, downgrade, or residualize claims whose simulation scope, fidelity, temporal semantics, resource bill, bottlenecks, omissions, or transfer decision do not support the stronger claim.
interfaces: Planning allocates budgets.
Routing chooses costed specialists.
Evidence measures cost-quality tradeoffs.
Serving infrastructure reports memory and throughput costs without converting them into quality claims.
Simulation records declare fidelity, resource bills, omitted variables, instrumentation effects, transfer decisions, and non-claims before synthetic results enter claim ledgers.
invariants: Budgets do not override protected safety gates.
High-risk tasks pay verification cost.
Cost savings are recorded with quality results.
Serving-throughput gains remain separate from verified-output and task-success claims.
Protected overhead is budgeted explicitly and cannot be silently deleted.
Displaced costs remain residuals until measured or accepted by a scoped evidence transition.
Simulation fidelity, scope, and resource bounds are declared before synthetic results support a claim.
Simulation results do not transfer beyond their supported claim boundary by default.
Approximation liberties, omitted variables, and instrumentation effects remain visible after results are summarized.
failure_modes: Aggregate serving throughput mistaken for lower single-request risk or better answer quality.
Simulation laundering: clean synthetic results described as if omitted variables, bottlenecks, and transfer limits were irrelevant.
Sandbox-to-world transfer without a declared fidelity and resource contract.
Parent-physics assumption drift without updating the simulation contract.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A resource ledger with budget, risk, cost, quality, verification tax, protected-overhead, displaced-cost, and evidence-reference fields, plus a companion Simulation Contract Record for synthetic or simulated results with scope, fidelity, temporal semantics, resource bill, bottlenecks, omissions, instrumentation effects, transfer decision, support-state effect, residual risks, and non-claims.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Resource economics matures into a risk-aware budget operating system and claim-transport layer for cognition. It decides when the stack may spend, defer, shrink, escalate, reject, residualize work, or downgrade synthetic evidence without letting local cost pressure delete verification, security, approval, replay, rollback, human review, fidelity limits, or transfer boundaries. In the mature budget OS, cognition has a budget and simulations have contracts: budgets cannot delete verification tax, protected overhead, review capacity, rollback cost, security cost, residual burden, or the requirement that simulated results travel only as far as declared scope, fidelity, resources, omissions, and transfer decisions allow.

proof_targets.target: A task budget cannot disable required safety or verification gates.
A high-risk task with insufficient verification budget is blocked or escalated.

The four-route costed-route fixture rejects the failed-verification and hidden-residual controls, keeps the bounded transform route eligible, proves it is lowest-cost among eligible modeled routes, and proves a finite selector-state replay ends with that route after rejecting the cheaper controls.

The finite Resource workflow trace fixture carries dispatch events whose costs, review minutes, and verification minutes roll up to the public summary, whose schedule keeps high-risk release work before lower-risk work, whose selected events preserve protected-overhead, residual-ownership, and non-claim guard flags, and whose public negative controls reject over-budget aggregate resource bills.

The finite capacity-smoothing reviewer trace fixture preserves bounded capacity, reviewer capacity, protected review overhead, displaced-review-cost residualization, no low-risk review during blocked protected review, and no support-state promotion; negative cases reject low-risk review hoarding, high-risk review without protected overhead, and missing displaced-cost residuals.

Aggregate serving-throughput or KV-cache reuse claims remain valid only when KV-cache budget, batching scope, and single-request verified-output boundaries are recorded; throughput-to-quality overclaims reject validity.

The finite governance-tax trade-off fixture carries 3 valid modeled scenarios, 5 expected-invalid controls, 2 governed selections, 1 allowed low-risk shortcut, protected-gate deletion rejection, residual-pricing requirement, reviewer-burden pricing, and no support-state or chapter-core promotion.
A simulation claim includes declared scope, fidelity, and resource bounds.
An experiment result cannot exceed the declared fidelity support of its simulation.

chapters.title: Mathematical and Search Substrates
chapters.problem: The source corpus contains exploratory mathematical/search substrates that may matter but must not be overclaimed.
chapters.insufficient: Novel substrates can become authority theater if they are not tied to baselines, adoption gates, and falsification criteria.
chapters.core_claim: Coils, calculi, and geometric search belong in the stack as optional specialist substrates until tests show where they improve search, routing, or compression.
mechanism: Frame each substrate as a specialist core or IR option.
Define expected advantage, baseline, and falsification condition.
Route experiments through evidence gates before adoption.
Distinguish backbone or sequence-substrate efficiency from draft-token acceleration and downstream task quality.
interfaces: Routing treats substrate as specialist.
Compression tests representation efficiency.
Evidence compares against baselines.
Fast-generation routes may consume substrate evidence, but adoption remains gated by substrate-specific A/B records.
invariants: Exploratory claims stay exploratory.
Baselines are recorded.
Failed hypotheses remain visible.
Backbone-efficiency claims do not imply search, routing, compression, or reasoning-quality gains.
failure_modes: Sequence-model throughput treated as evidence for unrelated substrate quality.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A technical-substrate appendix plan with experiment matrix, adoption gates, and a deterministic substrate-adoption trace that preserves baselines, negative controls, consumer-axis limits, fallback, retirement/refutation paths, residuals, and non-claim boundaries without claiming substrate usefulness.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Mathematical and search substrates need an adoption exchange before they can affect ordinary routes. It keeps unusual mathematics available for narrow specialist routes while making every adoption decision baseline-bound, falsifiable, consumer-gated, and reversible. In the mature substrate adoption exchange, mathematical substrates remain optional specialists until baseline-symmetric workloads show where they improve search, routing, compression, representation, and verifier workload without hiding tradeoffs.

chapters.title: Circle Calculus and Proof-Carrying AI Contracts
chapters.problem: The stack needs a way to turn selected mathematical and AI-infrastructure claims into theorem-linked, machine-readable contracts without treating proofs as model-quality evidence.
chapters.insufficient: Ordinary prose, tests, and diagrams can show intent, but they do not give downstream systems stable theorem IDs, proof status, receipt fingerprints, replay checks, and explicit non-claims.
chapters.core_claim: Proof-carrying AI contracts should package finite structural facts as theorem-linked receipts with deterministic fields, consumer gates, validation commands, and explicit non-claims.
mechanism: Model an engineering object as a finite circular address, schedule, window, or phase structure when that model is faithful.
Attach theorem IDs, proof status, dictionary IDs, fingerprints, deterministic fields, receipt or replay checks, and non-claims.
Require downstream workloads, ordinary baselines, negative controls, and metrics before any quality, runtime, memory, or transfer claim is promoted.
interfaces: Lean and proof manifests supply theorem status.
Python or CLI tools emit receipts and validation reports.
Theseus-style private experiments consume contracts without importing private results into public claims.
The evidence matrix records claim boundaries and support states.
invariants: Proof status is not a model-quality claim.
Python tests and diagrams are evidence, not formal proof.
Consumer readiness is not downstream success.
Non-claims survive transfer into later chapters.
failure_modes: Exact finite models mistaken for real-valued deployment behavior.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A contract record with theorem IDs, proof status, content fingerprint, deterministic fields, validation commands, consumer check, ordinary baselines, and non-claims.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: In its finished form, Circle proof-carrying contracts act as a proof-contract transport service. It moves finite structural facts through the ASI Stack with theorem references, deterministic fields, fingerprints, resolver state, replay commands, consumer gates, and non-claims intact. In the mature proof-contract transport service, proof-carrying AI contracts transport finite structural facts as theorem-linked receipts, while consumer gates preserve validation commands, fingerprints, resolver state, and explicit non-claims.

chapters.title: Coil Attention, Cyclic Memory, and Recurrence Contracts
chapters.problem: Memory, attention, and recurrence mechanisms need finite structural contracts for aliasing, coverage, freshness, active work, loop exits, and overthinking boundaries.
chapters.insufficient: Long context, sparse attention, ring buffers, and recursive loops can silently hide stale reads, uncovered lags, duplicate slots, alias collisions, or unbounded work.
chapters.core_claim: Coil attention and memory contracts should be used as structural guardrails for cyclic memory, sparse coverage, recurrence schedules, and work-budget admission, not as claims of retrieval or reasoning quality.
mechanism: Map memory slots, residue, winding, strides, windows, and recurrence schedules into finite circular structures.
Expose alias, freshness, coverage, active-token, work-saving, and loop-exit fields as auditable contract outputs.
Compare slot-only, slot-plus-winding, FIFO, LRU, content-gated, and ordinary attention or memory baselines before promoting performance claims.
interfaces: VCM context traces expose context adequacy and authority labels.
Attention and memory modules consume contract fields as diagnostic guardrails.
Benchmark adapters and Theseus transfer lanes decide whether structural facts survive actual workloads.
Proof contracts define the finite boundary of the claim.
invariants: Residue-only aliasing remains visible.
Full structural coverage is not semantic coverage.
Recurrence budgets include exits and guardrails.
Stale reads fail closed or enter residual escrow.
failure_modes: Freshness facts treated as retrieval-quality evidence.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A contract-backed memory and attention evaluation plan with slot, winding, coverage, freshness, active-token, loop-exit, work-budget, and baseline fields.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Coil memory is useful only when it becomes a memory-admission and recurrence-control plane. It makes slot, lag, freshness, aliasing, active-work, and loop-exit facts available to the stack without confusing those facts with retrieval quality or reasoning success. In the mature recurrence-control plane, coil memory provides structural guardrails for slots, lag, freshness, coverage, recurrence, and work budgets while leaving retrieval quality and reasoning quality to separate evidence.

chapters.title: CoilRA, MultiCoil RoPE, and Cyclic Mixers
chapters.problem: Position encodings, adapters, route heads, and mixers need a place for cyclic or block-cyclic structure that separates structural invariants from quality and runtime claims.
chapters.insufficient: Parameter-count, equivariance, or exact phase facts can be mistaken for better model behavior unless baselines, hardware costs, alias and load diagnostics, and failure cases are separated.
chapters.core_claim: CoilRA, MultiCoil RoPE, and cyclic mixers should be treated as optional specialist substrates whose structural guarantees must be paired with dense, LoRA, RoPE, learned, recurrent, or state-space baselines before adoption.
mechanism: Represent adapter blocks, block-cyclic routes, multiphase features, residue and winding, relative RoPE, and circulant convolution as structural contracts.
Attach alias, load, and parameter-accounting diagnostics before interpreting model behavior.
Measure quality, runtime, memory, and parameter tradeoffs on ordinary baselines and negative controls before promoting the substrate.
interfaces: Semantic representation chapters define when cyclic structure is actually present.
Routing heads or rankers may consume cyclic features only inside declared authority and evidence boundaries.
Resource-economics chapters account for parameters, kernels, memory, and latency.
Proof contracts and prototype experiments keep structural facts separate from performance results.
invariants: Equivariance is not model quality.
Parameter reduction is not an adoption proof.
Winding is not discarded when it is required to avoid aliasing.
Hardware-friendly sizes and kernel costs are recorded.
Real-valued RoPE claims are separated from exact integer phase-bank claims.
failure_modes: Mathematical elegance outruns kernels and baselines.
Exact finite proofs are overclaimed for real-valued RoPE deployments.
Lower parameter count comes with worse quality or runtime.
Phase aliases are hidden by residue-only diagnostics.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A mixer and position-substrate experiment matrix with structural proof references, ordinary baselines, quality/runtime/memory/parameter metrics, alias and load diagnostics, and explicit non-claims.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The endpoint for cyclic mixers is an evaluation lane, not an assumed upgrade path. It lets the stack test phase, recurrence, residue/winding, relative-position, circulant, and block-cyclic ideas without spending structural proof as if it were quality, runtime, memory, or deployment evidence. In the mature cyclic-substrate evaluation lane, cyclic mixers are optional specialist substrates until structural receipts, diagnostics, hardware constraints, and baseline-symmetric tradeoff packets justify a scoped canary route.

parts.title: Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book
parts.part_job: Turn the architecture into an accountable research program: executable specifications, Lean proof envelopes, benchmark ratchets, reference traces, implementation roadmap, living-book method, and bibliography discipline.

chapters.title: Executable Specifications and Lean Proof Envelope
chapters.problem: The book needs to decide which architecture claims should become executable specs or Lean proofs.
chapters.insufficient: Formal methods are ineffective when applied to vague philosophical claims instead of operational predicates and transitions.
chapters.core_claim: Lean proofs and executable specs should target small invariants: authority, support states, SCF replacement, job lifecycle, context adequacy, and residual records.

chapters.minimal_implementation: A proof manifest, proof target schema and fixture, Lean workspace, proof-readiness validator, and proof artifact traceability audit report for all implemented finite-record targets.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The formal-methods endpoint is a claims control plane for the whole stack. It does not try to prove "ASI safety" or "alignment" as one grand theorem. It maintains a live registry of every formal-looking promise, routes each promise to the right artifact lane, and refuses to let a passed artifact support a broader claim than it actually checks. A mature proof envelope makes every chapter, protocol, release, and runtime module consume proof only through a typed Proof Target Record. The record would name the exact predicate, source claim, artifact lane, verifier command, verifier result, semantic adequacy review, consumer requirements, support-state effect, limitations, and non-claims. A Lean predicate, JSON Schema, process validator, behavior test, benchmark, external theorem reference, and research backlog item would remain different objects with different authority.

chapters.title: Benchmark Ratchets and Anti-Goodhart Evidence
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a way to move capability claims through evidence without overfitting to fixed benchmarks.
chapters.insufficient: Benchmarks can saturate, be gamed, erase regressions, or fail to represent real requirements.
chapters.core_claim: Benchmark ratchets should preserve regressions, create harder frontiers, record residuals, and resist Goodhart pressure.

chapters.minimal_implementation: A benchmark ratchet record with frontier, mastery, residuals, regressions, and anti-Goodhart checks.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The benchmark endpoint is an operating system for evaluation, not a leaderboard appendix. Every evaluation would become a governed evidence packet with enough history to tell whether the system learned a capability, learned a test, leaked a public calibration surface, or merely improved a proxy. A mature benchmark OS manages benchmarks as instruments with lifecycle state. A task can begin as a candidate metric, become a frontier probe, graduate into a regression floor, enter contamination review, be retired, or remain blocked. Success would never move a claim by itself; it would open a promotion review that compares baselines, residuals, negative results, holdouts, transfer checks, mutation checks, public-calibration limits, and the exact claim being considered.

chapters.title: Policy Optimization and Learning from Feedback
chapters.problem: A governed stack needs to convert feedback, verification, benchmark pressure, and failure into better future behavior without allowing reward signals to bypass evidence, authority, or rollback boundaries.
chapters.insufficient: RLHF, preference optimization, and verifier rewards are often treated as model-alignment recipes rather than governed update mechanisms across planners, routers, context policies, execution policies, and generation modes.
chapters.core_claim: Policy optimization is the stack's learning actuator: it can update planners, routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution policies, and generators, but governance decides which feedback is admissible and whether any update may be promoted.

mechanism: Classify the target policy: planner, VCM, router, generator, verifier, execution, governance, or generation mode.
Record the feedback source: human preference, verifier reward, benchmark result, tool result, context-grounding check, governance check, or latency/resource signal.
Choose a training/update family by the policy being optimized, the feedback available, and the failure mode that must be blocked.
Constrain the update with drift bounds, evidence gates, rollback plans, and residual accounting.
Evaluate for task success, regressions, reward hacking, authority compliance, and cost before promotion.
interfaces: Benchmarks and verifiers produce reward or preference signals.
VCM and artifact graphs preserve the training/evaluation context and evidence references.
Talos records training, tool, and evaluation artifacts.
Spinoza and UAT audit reward meaning, verifier failures, and reward-hacking probes.
SCF gates decide promotion, quarantine, rollback, or continued experiment status.
invariants: Reward is not evidence unless the verifier and evaluation boundary are recorded.
Policy updates cannot expand authority by training side effect.
A faster or shorter policy is not promoted unless task success and regressions are preserved.
Reward hacking probes are part of the update record, not optional commentary.
Rollback remains available for promoted updates.
failure_modes: Over-optimization for preference style rather than truth or task success.
Policy drift crossing SCF authority or readiness boundaries.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A policy optimization record schema, fixture, and finite Lean predicates that separate feedback, update constraints, evaluation, governance gates, rollback, and non-claims from unrun training results.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The learning endpoint is a governed actuator for behavior change across the entire stack. Policy optimization would not be a fine-tuning side quest attached to the generator. It would be the controlled mechanism by which planners, routers, context selectors, verifiers, execution policies, generation modes, and governance assistants become better without gaining new authority by training side effect. A mature policy-update lane treats each update as a behavior-change lease. The lease would name the target policy, old and new behavior, admissible feedback, reward or preference boundary, update family, drift bound, holdouts, regressions, reward-hacking probes, authority effect, rollback plan, monitor window, deployment scope, evidence packets, residuals, and non-claims. PPO-like, DPO-like, GRPO-like, RLVR-like, context-policy, router-policy, or custom control-policy updates would be implementation choices under this record, not substitutes for governance.

proof_targets.target: An admitted policy update records target layer, reward signal, update constraint, evaluation refs, governance gates, and rollback plan.
A policy update with unverified reward or missing governance gate cannot be promoted.

A policy-promotion route rejects or routes updates with inadmissible feedback, missing target evaluation, missing holdout or contamination checks, missing reward-hacking probes, governance or authority gaps, missing rollback, or regression/residual gaps before promotion.

The deterministic policy-update lease probe records six synthetic routing samples, five candidate policies, selected canary kept experimental, expected-invalid controls, rollback dry run, no support-state effect, and non-claim boundary.

chapters.title: Artifact Steward Agents and Living Project Governance
chapters.problem: Durable artifacts need continuity across planning, funding, work assignment, release, maintenance, governance, and eventual sunset without turning an AI manager into the owner of the project.
chapters.insufficient: Issue trackers, CI systems, bots, DAOs, funding pages, and project-management tools each cover a slice of artifact lifecycle, but none by themselves preserve mission, memory, authority, treasury limits, contributor credit, evidence, and death protocol as one governed project steward.
chapters.core_claim: Every durable artifact should be able to carry a bounded steward agent that preserves mission, memory, roadmap, budget, contributors, compute, evidence, governance, and sunset policy across the artifact lifecycle while acting only through explicit work contracts, verification gates, treasury limits, contribution ledgers, and governance rules.

mechanism: Bind a steward to an artifact mission, non-goals, authority ceiling, governance model, budget policy, evidence requirements, and sunset criteria.
Run an explicit lifecycle from inception to bootstrap, build, release, maintenance, governance, decline, and archive instead of treating stewardship as one long task list.
Convert roadmap goals into project work contracts with required outputs, allowed tools, forbidden tools, worker requirements, budget, verification, payment, and reputation fields.
Orchestrate humans, agents, personal hives, project hives, CI runners, rented compute, reviewers, and maintainers through typed jobs and evidence bundles.
Use treasury policy and event-taint records to keep spending and untrusted repository events inside explicit review boundaries.
Separate reputation, governance shares, economic credits, authorship, review credit, and evidence credit so contribution, voting, money, and truth claims cannot collapse into one gameable token.
Move across manual, assisted, bounded-autonomous, community-governed, and sunset modes only through charter, policy, evidence, rollback, and approval records.
Treat webhooks, issues, pull requests, comments, worker outputs, benchmark artifacts, and release events as typed and tainted intake until the steward records review.
interfaces: VCM preserves project memory, source documents, decisions, issues, releases, and unresolved questions.
PlanForge and Talos turn goals into milestones, issues, jobs, reviews, artifacts, and releases.
Spinoza, Benchmaxxing, and evidence ledgers decide whether submitted work changes claim or readiness state.
TokenMana and treasury policies bound spending, compute rental, bounties, and recurring operations.
SCF, governance rights, and constitutional predicates prevent steward authority from replacing owner, maintainer, contributor, or community authority.
invariants: The steward is not the owner.
Mission, non-goals, and sunset criteria remain durable.
Spending is a bounded actuator governed by treasury policy.
Untrusted issues, pull requests, comments, and worker outputs enter as tainted context until reviewed.
Major governance changes require explicit proposal, review, delay, quorum, appeal, or guardian paths.
A project that no longer has value, users, maintainers, or funds can enter sunset mode rather than continuing activity for its own sake.
failure_modes: Mission drift from optimizing activity rather than purpose.
Treasury drain through low-value compute, bounty spam, or runaway recurring costs.
Governance capture by large holders, early maintainers, or steward-controlled scoring.
Agentic workflow injection through untrusted repository event context.
AI maintainer monopoly that makes the project opaque to humans.
Zombie project continuation after user value disappears.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A `PROJECT_STEWARD.yml` file plus a GitHub-oriented steward bot that labels issues, drafts plans, proposes work contracts, requests CI, summarizes pull requests, writes release notes, records evidence, opens sunset reviews, and never spends funds or merges to protected branches without policy approval.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: A durable-artifact stack ultimately needs a project-lifecycle operating system. The steward would not be a project owner, treasurer, maintainer monopoly, or autonomous manager with implied rights. It would be a bounded continuity service that keeps mission, memory, roadmap, work, funds, compute, contributors, evidence, releases, governance, and sunset criteria inspectable across many human and AI work cycles. A mature steward OS would let every durable artifact carry an Artifact Steward Charter plus typed records for work contracts, contribution ledgers, treasury policy, event taint, steward action decisions, release evidence, federation contracts, and sunset review. The steward would propose, prepare, coordinate, and execute only inside explicit authority. Humans, maintainers, communities, project hives, personal hives, CI runners, and rented compute would receive scoped contracts rather than inheriting project authority.

proof_targets.target: A steward-managed work contract records objective, authority, allowed tools, forbidden tools, verification requirements, budget, and non-claims before dispatch.
A steward action that exceeds treasury policy, changes governance rules, or touches protected assets cannot execute without explicit approval evidence.
A stewarded release publication requires test, evidence, changelog, residual, and approval records.
When sunset criteria are met, ordinary work generation is blocked until a sunset review is opened.

A steward lifecycle route sends tainted unreviewed events to quarantine, sunset criteria without review to sunset review, autonomy escalation without charter approval to approval, and over-policy treasury spend to approval.

A steward contribution ledger keeps authorship, review, evidence, compensation, reputation, governance effect, and conflicts separated; collapsed governance scoring is rejected and support-state changes require evidence-transition records.
A steward federation contract requires scoped work contracts, bounded worker authority, tool/data/budget gates, external-spend approval, and evidence-bundle requirements before dispatch.

open_evidence_gaps: Appendix C now records exact passage-reviewed mappings for all assigned Artifact Steward Agents sources; support remains at argument until a steward bot, treasury executor, event-taint workflow, contributor-ledger service, governance runner, release gate, sunset protocol, or behavioral tests exist.

Record-shape schemas, valid fixtures, narrow finite Lean predicates, the benchmark anti-Goodhart synthetic release evidence/approval handoff, and the deterministic public-safe Artifact steward lifecycle probe are implemented. The lifecycle probe checks `valid_clean_release_review_proposal`, `valid_sunset_review_route`, and six expected-invalid controls over fixture-composed route records, but no steward bot, treasury policy engine, event-taint workflow, governance system, contributor-ledger service, project federation harness, release runner, or sunset protocol has been executed from this repository.

External tooling and governance sources are reviewed as adjacent substrate/risk references only; they do not prove the ASI Stack steward design or any safety, legal, funding, governance, or capture-resistance property.

mechanism: Trace user intent through constitution, governance, planning, VCM, routing, verification, execution, evidence, and improvement.
Show the compression loop embedded inside the broader governance, memory, routing, and verification stack.
Show which artifact each layer emits.
Identify where authority can stop or reroute the process.

SCF gates control improvement.

chapters.minimal_implementation: A reference flow diagram, interface table, and deterministic reference-trace fixture set that validates record shape, parent artifacts, authority/evidence/residual deltas, blocked-path stop conditions, validation commands, support-state non-promotion, and explicit non-claims without claiming an integrated runtime.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The integration endpoint is a trace kernel for the whole ASI Stack. The stack would not be a diagram of cooperating layers; it would be an inspectable machine that can explain what authority entered, which artifacts were produced, what evidence changed, which residuals survived, and why a path was promoted, blocked, narrowed, rolled back, or escalated. A mature reference trace kernel would make every significant run emit a Reference Trace Record. The record would connect human intent, constitutional authority, command contracts, plan DAGs, VCM packets, route decisions, claim envelopes, work orders, execution logs, proof/spec receipts, benchmark ratchets, policy-update leases, steward decisions, SCF transitions, release notes, and residual ledgers. The reference architecture would be judged by whether those records survive happy paths and blocked paths, not by how coherent the diagram looks.

chapters.title: Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference
chapters.problem: The book needs a concrete implementation-reference chapter for how the ASI stack can be operated as report-first local machinery instead of only conceptual layer diagrams.
chapters.insufficient: A prototype roadmap is not enough unless readers can see how goals, plans, arms, reports, gates, residuals, checkpoints, compute, and operator surfaces interact in a working research system.
chapters.core_claim: Project Theseus should be mined as a report-first implementation reference for RMI, showing how pressure, residuals, gates, ledgers, operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution governance can instantiate the ASI stack without promoting local results as general capability claims.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports mining Project Theseus as a report-first RMI implementation reference with current-state caveats, pressure loops, residual escrow, benchmark floors, bounded specialist routing, sparse teacher governance, checkpoints, Hive nodes, safety boundaries, and machine-readable reports as the contract surface.

mechanism: Treat reports and ledgers as the contract, with dashboards as surfaces over those artifacts.
Use the loop pressure -> attempt -> residual -> diagnosis -> compression -> verification -> structure -> new pressure as implementation discipline.
Gate heavy training and self-evolution through architecture, preflight, resource, and candidate-promotion checks.
Route operator work through a durable work board, command vocabulary, node registry, hooks, and feedback reports.
Keep sparse teacher use proposal-first and guarded by branches, checks, benchmark regressions, and review.
interfaces: The plan compiler supplies contracts, semantic IR DAGs, VCM slices, routes, claim targets, and replay traces.
Octopus arms, SymLiquid CGS, SparkStream control, and Hive runtime map stack layers into reports and configs.
Genesis-style artifact kernels preserve claims, critiques, benchmark results, tool promotions, architecture decisions, and feedback.
Capability matrices and benchmark ledgers expose readiness and residuals without turning local reports into public empirical proof.
invariants: The frontier moves while the regression floor holds.
Promotion uses external-inference-zero evidence where the local gate requires it.
Reports survive the run and are not replaced by dashboard prose.
Private experiments and public calibration remain separated.
Unknown task kinds are blocked rather than guessed.
Architecture changes follow the intervention ladder.
failure_modes: Remote work proceeds without allowlists, TTLs, or kill switches.
Local prototype results are overclaimed as public evidence.
chapters.minimal_implementation: An implementation-reference crosswalk table from ASI stack layer to Theseus report, config, or tool surface, with evidence state and public claim boundary for each row.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: Project Theseus becomes valuable here only as a public-safe implementation-reference lab. Project Theseus would remain useful to this book only if its reports, configs, gates, ledgers, work-board entries, residuals, and replay notes can be mined without turning private or local runs into general capability claims. A mature Theseus reference lane would provide importable report bundles rather than dashboard impressions. Each bundle would identify the ASI Stack layer it exercises, the command or report source, configuration, environment boundary, artifact checksum, gate result, residual, missing artifact, public claim boundary, and non-claims. A dashboard could visualize those bundles, but the report bundle would be the evidence object.

proof_targets.target: An implementation-reference claim names the report, config, or tool surface and does not rely on dashboard prose alone.
A capability or self-evolution promotion is blocked when required gate reports are absent or failing.

A public-safe Theseus report-bundle audit fixture is accepted only when bundle components, replay-readiness rows, crosswalk rows, gate mappings, work-board contract fields, visible artifact gaps, intervention-ladder ordering, and no-promotion boundaries are complete.

A public-safe Theseus public task-bundle import fixture is accepted only when source reports, metadata-only public task boundaries, zero public training rows, zero exported prompts/tests/solutions/traces/scores/candidate code, operator and benchmark gates, visible artifact gaps, no clean-live-replay claim, and no-promotion boundaries are complete.

The selected Theseus/Fast support-lane aggregate carries 4 command replays, 16 tracked artifacts, 68 public task records, 14 expected-invalid or rejected controls, 2 accepted no-promotion decisions, the four included support surfaces, and explicit no-clean-live-replay, no-model-quality, no-generation-speed, no-useful-solution-model, no-chapter-core-promotion, and no-support-state-promotion boundaries.

chapters.title: Prototype Roadmap
chapters.problem: The architecture needs a build sequence that does not introduce agency or self-improvement before auditability and governance are credible.
chapters.insufficient: A roadmap that jumps to autonomous improvement skips source matrices, artifact graphs, claim ledgers, tests, and authority controls.
chapters.core_claim: The prototype should grow in staged increments from source matrix and artifact graph to governed capability replacement.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports starting the roadmap with durable artifacts, command contracts, claim ledgers, residuals, feedback, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage before higher-agency layers are introduced.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove that any roadmap phase has been implemented, accepted, validated, or used to promote a capability claim.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for staged minimum viable VIEA, durable artifacts, command contracts, claim/support-state discipline, critique and release records, residuals, benchmark/regression coverage, and the small first vertical slice before broader specialists, workflow compilation, ratchets, and runtime adapters; no completed roadmap phase is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports phasing benchmark frontier work after ledgers, baselines, wall diagnosis, regression floors, anti-Goodhart safeguards, and residual accounting exist.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No benchmark harness, phase acceptance benchmark, mutation test, holdout run, contamination audit, empirical run, or phase acceptance result is recorded here.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for benchmark frontier-to-regression phasing, residual diagnosis, anti-Goodhart safeguards, mutation, hidden holdouts, benchmark/model ledgers, architecture-change caution, and the staged implementation phases for benchmark ledgers, model ledgers, residual analysis, diagnostic ladders, and frontier expansion; no benchmark run or acceptance result is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove field replacement is safe, reversible, production-ready, evaluator-sound, authority-complete, or rollback-successful.

mechanism: Start with source inventory, claim ledger, artifact graph, and validation.
Add planner, VCM, typed execution, verification, benchmark ratchets, and SCF gates in order.
Delay recursive self-improvement until evaluator and governance integrity are credible.
interfaces: Each phase unlocks a later layer.
Evidence gates decide promotion.
Changelog and releases document progress.
invariants: Phases have acceptance criteria.
Self-improvement is gated.
Roadmap milestones are not test results.
failure_modes: Skipping verification because prototypes feel useful.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A phase table with deliverables, gates, blockers, and acceptance criteria.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The roadmap endpoint is a phase-gated build controller for the ASI Stack. The roadmap would not be a list of aspirations. It would be a dependency graph that decides which prototypes can be built, which are blocked, which are allowed only as research demos, and which may affect claim support after evidence review. A mature roadmap controller gives every phase explicit prerequisites, acceptance gates, commands, required artifacts, evidence refs, residuals, unlocks, phase debt, retirement criteria, and non-claims. A later phase could not borrow credibility from an earlier milestone unless the acceptance record exists and the downstream dependency matches the evidence boundary.

codex_tests.name: Phase acceptance checklist
codex_tests.purpose: Check that dependent phases have required artifacts, acceptance gates, evidence refs, residual handling, and non-claim boundaries before integration.
codex_tests.status: implemented by prototype phase gate harness; validated locally by synthetic fixtures; no phase completion or support-state promotion
codex_tests.result_status: validated locally by synthetic fixtures; no phase completion or support-state promotion

codex_tests.name: Dependency gate review
codex_tests.purpose: Check that dependency order, self-improvement evaluator gates, phase debt retirement conditions, and support-promotion evidence-transition gates block invalid roadmap advancement.
codex_tests.status: implemented by prototype phase gate harness; validated locally by synthetic fixtures; no deployed build controller or support-state promotion
codex_tests.result_status: validated locally by synthetic fixtures; no deployed build controller or support-state promotion

codex_tests.name: Prototype evidence-state audit
codex_tests.purpose: Check that expired or stale evidence routes to rerun/reject state with residual escrow instead of canary/default promotion.
codex_tests.status: implemented by readiness/residual gate harness; validated locally; full phase acceptance audit not run
codex_tests.result_status: validated locally by synthetic fixtures; full phase acceptance audit not run

proof_targets.target: A roadmap phase can unlock a dependent phase only after acceptance gates pass.
A phase milestone cannot promote a claim without evidence artifacts.

A modeled prototype phase-gate fixture bridge accepts only the complete public-safe fixture summary and rejects missing non-claim boundaries while preserving no support-state promotion and no phase-completion claim.

chapters.title: Living Book Methodology
chapters.problem: The book itself must remain a living technical system rather than a static anthology.
chapters.insufficient: Static manuscripts cannot show source additions, claim-state movement, deprecations, proof updates, render status, or test history.
chapters.core_claim: The living book should model the ASI stack discipline through manifest-driven Quarto, source queues, claim/evidence matrices, proof manifests, tests, changelogs, and releases.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports treating book improvement as a ratchet with lifecycle states, wall diagnosis, ledgers, residuals, regression preservation, anti-Goodhart safeguards, and architecture-change discipline.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not prove manuscript quality, benchmark validity, editorial completeness, rendered-site quality, or any chapter claim.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for benchmark lifecycle, frontier-to-regression ratcheting, residual reporting, benchmark/model ledgers, anti-Goodhart safeguards, architecture-change discipline, and implementation phases; useful as living-book improvement discipline, not as manuscript-quality evidence.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the living-book method through durable artifacts, claim support states, residuals, verified outputs, feedback, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not make the book itself an implemented VIEA runtime, prove the architecture claims, prove source interpretation quality, or validate reader/audio editions.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for artifact graph, claim/support-state ledger, workflow-to-tool compiler, residuals, regression coverage, release manifest, feedback records, low-friction sidecar artifact/claim files, and non-overclaiming implementation roadmap; no VIEA runtime or book-claim truth is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports the value of explicit build, test, bridge, training, and resource-constrained workflows as a cautionary prototype-lineage example for living technical documentation.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Speculative and unbuilt in this repo; no BugBrain implementation, build, emulation, flash, bridge, benchmark, hardware result, AGI claim, or consciousness claim is validated.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for explicit build commands, pretraining/emulation, flashing, bridge interaction, training/testing workflow, resource constraints, and speculative consciousness/AGI framing; only the documentation-discipline lesson is used here.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports proceduralizing repeated book-maintenance workflows through trajectory logging, loop detection, tool synthesis, verification, routing, monitoring, and retirement discipline.
claim_source_mappings.limits: No autonomous loop-closure system, trajectory-mining run, tool-synthesis harness, automated book-maintenance router, runtime monitor, or retirement workflow has been run; book workflow reuse remains process discipline.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for repeated trajectory logging, loop detection, parameter discovery, tool synthesis, verification contracts, registry fields, routing, monitoring, lifecycle/retirement, and phase roadmap; the book uses this as process discipline, not proof of an autonomous loop-closure implementation.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports using runtime-reference ledgers, replay, readiness gates, residuals, handoff, and promotion blockers as analogues for living-book release and evidence discipline.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Reviewed source note only because authenticated connector text is not published here; MoECOT reports, runtime artifacts, ledgers, replay, readiness gates, and benchmarks are not reproduced here and do not prove book quality.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for compact orchestration, specialist lanes, fail-closed ledgers, readiness gates, replay, promotion blockers, and source-reported limitations; no MoECOT artifact or benchmark is promoted.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports terminology normalization and variant comparison for MoECOT-related living-book updates.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Variant source only; not independent evidence, not a reproduced artifact, and not a separate support-state basis.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for Markdown export status, terminology normalization, section recovery, and variant comparison; it is not counted as independent corroboration beyond the primary MoECOT source.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports keeping the living book honest about source-reported status, remaining work, unreproduced benchmarks, promotion blockers, and implementation sequencing.
claim_source_mappings.limits: Reviewed source note only because the local cache is an auth-gate placeholder; roadmap/status context only, and reported commands, benchmark results, readiness state, and implementation status are not locally reproduced.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed source note only for remaining-work/status context, source-reported commands/results caveat, promotion blockers, implementation sequencing, and conservative support-state rule; no reported result is promoted.

mechanism: Use `book_structure.json` for order and dynamic chapters.
Use `docs/book_outline.md` for drafting, source queues, and Lean scope.
Treat conversation-mined packets as author-intent and lineage context, not as independent evidence.
Use drafting annotations such as [SOURCE], [AUTHOR INTENT], [SYNTHESIS], [EXPERIMENT], and [OPEN] in private notes or source notes when helpful.
Require meaningful updates to sync scaffold, proof manifest, validation, render, and changelog.
interfaces: Source ingestion feeds source notes.
Drafting feeds claim matrices.
Tests feed support states.
Releases feed GitHub Pages.
invariants: No fabricated source content.
No fabricated test results.
Deprecated claims remain visible.
failure_modes: Public site diverges from Quarto source.
Readers cannot tell which claims are speculative.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A public Quarto repo with dynamic scaffold, source matrix, claim matrix, proof manifest, validation, and GitHub Pages.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The endpoint is a research operating system that happens to render as a book. The live site would remain the canonical, AI-readable, researcher-auditable surface, while Human view, reader editions, ebook formats, and future audio scripts would be derived projections with explicit strip policies and release records. A mature living-book workflow would let a future agent ingest a new AI paper, mine its public-safe content, decide whether to update a chapter or add a precise new one, update the outline and manifest, generate proof/test backlog, draft or revise prose, validate source/evidence boundaries, render the live site, and prepare reader editions without hand-renumbering or hidden support-state changes.

chapters.title: Open Research Agenda and Bibliography Plan
chapters.problem: The book needs a managed research backlog and bibliography plan so new papers can be inserted without destabilizing the architecture.
chapters.insufficient: A pile of sources or ad hoc citations does not tell future writing agents what to load, compare, prove, test, or defer.
chapters.core_claim: The bibliography and research agenda should map source families, external literature queues, missing papers, proof targets, experiment backlogs, and chapter insertion rules.

claim_source_mappings.mapped_support: Supports a research backlog for context adequacy, semantic-unit verification, contradiction-rate tests, summary-loss accounting, and the distinction between long context and verified reasoning.
claim_source_mappings.limits: The proposed tests, theorem sketches, contradiction-rate claims, and adequacy claims remain source-proposed until mechanized or empirically tested.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for context-window versus verification-workspace distinction, semantic units, pairwise constraint checking, summary-loss accounting, contradiction-rate proposals, and proposed tests; useful as backlog scope, not as a run or theorem result.

claim_source_mappings.limits: Does not provide local benchmark results, benchmark-harness validation, mutation/holdout runs, contamination audits, or citation-normalized external literature by itself.
claim_source_mappings.passage_review_note: Reviewed local raw cache for benchmark lifecycle, wall diagnosis, residual reports, anti-Goodhart safeguards, benchmark/model ledgers, implementation phases, and frontier/regression summary; no local benchmark result or citation-normalized literature is promoted.

mechanism: Maintain source inventory, source notes, the Corben corpus and local-project appendix, the separate external-literature appendix, and source-loading queues.

Track recovered and missing items separately: Field of God AI Constitution, Circle Calculus, and Project Theseus/SymLiquid are now local/public project sources; Genesis Engine, Genesis Foundry, BBVCA detail, Spinoza detail, VCM review conflicts, and private empirical results remain recovery or evidence-backlog items.
Use triage rules to update chapters or add precise new ones.
interfaces: Appendix G is Corben's own papers, Corben-supplied materials, recovered project records, and local project records, while Appendix H is a separate appendix for external sources by other authors and organizations.
Source notes support chapter drafting.
Book outline tells future agents what to mine.
invariants: Do not cite unread sources as support.
Do not infer bibliographic metadata.
New papers update inventory before chapter claims.
failure_modes: Duplicate chapter creation for overlapping ideas.
chapters.minimal_implementation: A split Corben-source-corpus and external-literature plan with source-note backlog and insertion/merge rules.
chapters.beyond_state_of_art: The bibliography endpoint is a source and research graph for the entire ASI Stack. The bibliography would not be an afterthought at the end of the book, and it would not blur Corben's papers, Corben-supplied materials, recovered project records, or local project sources with outside literature. It would be the routing table that tells future agents which ideas have sources, which sources have been read, which chapters they affect, which proof or experiment backlogs they create, which material is external, and which claims still cannot move. A mature source graph would give every paper, project, local note, external reference, conversation-mined packet, recovery item, and missing source a public-safety policy, ingestion state, source note, chapter assignments, deduplication status, evidence-transition preconditions, proof/test backlog, citation status, and non-claims. New material would update the research graph before it changes manuscript prose.
